The mean prevalence of infection was reduced from 63.5% at baseline (range 26.3% to 85.7%) to 11.5% six months after the first treatment range (0.0 to 24.4%) as previously described (P<0.0001). The next three biannual distributions continued to reduce the prevalence to 2.6% (range 0.0% to 7.0%), a significant decrease from after a single treatment (P = 0.0004). From 6 to 24 months after the last treatment (the 24 and 42 month visits), infection had returned to an average of 25.2% (range 2.0% to 70.0%).
The 16 villages varied widely in their pre-treatment and post-treatment prevalence, and in how rapidly infection returned after treatments had been discontinued (Figure 2).
74.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that infection returns after treatment discontinuation in trachoma-endemic communities. Specifically, it states that infection increased in 14 of 16 villages in the 24 months after the last treatment, and that the mean prevalence returned to 25.2% after 24 months. This indicates that even after four biannual distributions, the infection resurges, though at a lower level than the baseline. The option correctly infers that mass antibiotic distributions cannot be discontinued without the risk of resurgence, which is directly supported by the observed pattern of infection returning after treatment cessation. The variation in return rates across villages further supports the idea that resurgence is predictable but variable in timing and magnitude.

76.Option: The higher total costs associated with P. vivax infections compared to other Plasmodium species may be explained by longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, and higher caregiver involvement due to the younger age profile of affected patients.
Evidence: P. vivax patients presented with the highest total costs at $199.05 (SD=173.87; Table 4). In contrast to other malaria species, most of the P. vivax costs were direct costs (63%) where hospital and clinic out-of-pocket costs were double the direct costs of malaria due to other Plasmodium species (Table E in S1 Text).
76.Rationale: The passage states that P. vivax had the highest total costs, but attributes this to higher direct costs (63% of total) related to hospital and clinic out-of-pocket expenses, not to longer treatment duration, more frequent clinic visits, or caregiver involvement. The option incorrectly attributes the cost difference to factors not mentioned in the passage. The passage does not mention the age profile of P. vivax patients as a contributing factor to costs.

76.Option: The use of standardized wage estimates from national surveys to value productivity losses resulted in a 78% increase in total cost per episode, indicating that the base case underestimates the true economic burden of malaria on households.
Evidence: When using the Malaysia household inequities survey for Sabah was utilized (US$389.21 per month) [31], the total cost increased by 78% (US$236.03) when applied to all adults in Scenario 4 and 104% (US$270.41) when applied to all patients in Scenario 5.
76.Rationale: The passage states that using standardized wage estimates from national surveys (specifically the Malaysia Household Inequities Survey) resulted in a 78% increase in total cost per episode. However, the passage does not state that the base case underestimates the true economic burden. The base case uses self-reported wages with imputation for missing data, which may be more accurate for the specific population. The option makes a value judgment about the base case that is not supported by the passage.

76.Option: Households in Sabah face a disproportionate financial burden from malaria due to the high prevalence of zoonotic P. knowlesi transmission among working-age males in rural agricultural areas, which increases both direct and indirect costs.
Evidence: P. knowlesi is most prevalent in males aged between 20 and 40 years in Malaysia, who are the most commonly reported demographic in formal employment [15,18,19].

More than half of P. knowlesi cases nationally have been attributed to agriculture and plantation-based work activities, with many of the remaining cases linked to timber logging and forestry work [22].
The mean total household cost per malaria episode was US$130.53 (SD=102.24) with a median of $96.11 (IQR=$70.73, $153.01) (Table 3).
Working-age males reported higher indirect costs at US$95.66, compared to US$52.62 for others (p<0.001).
76.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that P. knowlesi is most prevalent in working-age males in rural agricultural areas, and that these cases contribute to higher household costs. The higher indirect costs for working-age males, combined with the high prevalence of P. knowlesi in this demographic, supports the inference that households in Sabah face a disproportionate financial burden from malaria due to zoonotic P. knowlesi transmission in this population.

76.Option: Severe malaria cases, all due to P. knowlesi infection, were associated with substantially higher indirect costs, primarily due to longer hospital stays and greater productivity losses among working-age males.
Evidence: The seven severe malaria cases (all P. knowlesi) had 91% higher total costs than non-severe cases (US$239.64 versus US$125.27 respectively, p=0.006) (Table 4), predominantly due to substantially higher indirect costs (US$89.25 mean difference) for severe malaria.

The average number of days lost to illness increased to 13.9 (SD = 15.6) for severe P. knowlesi cases, comparatively higher than non-severe P. knowlesi cases at 8.3 days (SD = 6.5, p = 0.141) and all non-zoonotic malaria (P. falciparum, P. vivax and P. malariae) cases at 10.4 days (SD = 5.80, p = 0.477).
The length of hospitalization for severe P. knowlesi cases (mean = 5.6 days, SD = 1.4) was higher than for non-severe malaria cases due to any species (mean = 3.9, SD = 1.4, p=0.004).
76.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that severe P. knowlesi cases had higher total costs, primarily due to higher indirect costs. These higher indirect costs are attributed to longer hospital stays (5.6 days vs 3.9 days) and greater productivity losses (13.9 days lost vs 8.3 days for non-severe cases). The option correctly identifies the relationship between severe P. knowlesi cases and increased indirect costs.

76.Option: Despite the provision of free health-facility-related costs of malaria care by the Ministry of Health, Malaysia, patients in Sabah faced substantial out-of-pocket costs for a single malaria episode, with a mean of $131, and a large proportion of households experienced catastrophic health expenditure, particularly when using lower income thresholds.
Evidence: Malaysia has free universal access to malaria screening, testing, and treatment through public health facilities [6,7].
The mean total household cost per malaria episode was US$130.53 (SD=102.24) with a median of $96.11 (IQR=$70.73, $153.01) (Table 3).

When using the mean monthly self-reported income value of US$148, direct health expenditure for a single malaria episode accounted for 27% of monthly income. Eighty percent (122/152) of patients reported direct health costs above 10% of monthly income, indicating catastrophic health expenditure.
76.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that Malaysia provides free malaria care through public health facilities, yet patients still faced substantial out-of-pocket costs (mean of $130.53). It also provides evidence that 80% of patients experienced catastrophic health expenditure when using the self-reported income threshold. This directly supports the inference that despite free clinical care, households in Sabah face significant financial burdens from malaria.

78.Option: The persistence of clinical signs of trachoma after the clearance of C. trachomatis infection indicates that the current WHO guidelines for trachoma elimination are ineffective in preventing long-term ocular damage.
Evidence: The correlation between disease and infection is not particularly strong and tends to weaken as the community prevalence drops [5], [6].

At five years TT was found in 6/456 (1.3%) aged 15 years and over. Two were incident cases (compared with baseline), three had TT at baseline (one of which had received surgery) and one was a new resident at five years.
There was a marked reduction in observed fly-eye contacts at the time of examination in 1 to 9 year olds from 18.0% (95% CI 14.7 to 21.5) at baseline to 1.5% (95% CI 0.5–2.5) at 5 years.
78.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that WHO guidelines are ineffective in preventing long-term ocular damage. The persistence of clinical signs after infection clearance is explained by the weakening correlation between infection and disease as prevalence drops, not by guideline ineffectiveness. The passage also notes that TT prevalence remained below 0.1% in most villages, which aligns with WHO's Ultimate Intervention Goals. The reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces further supports that environmental improvements are contributing to disease control.

78.Option: The lack of a control group in the study design means that the observed decline in C. trachomatis infection cannot be attributed to mass drug administration, and therefore antibiotic treatment should not be used in low prevalence trachoma settings.
Evidence: The study population was comprised of all the individuals living in a geographically defined area 3km by 4km in size in Upper Saloum District, The Gambia.
Antibiotic treatment coverage at baseline was 83.3% (95% CI: 81.4–85.1) of the study area population and 91.3% (95% CI: 89.0–93.7) of 1 to 9 year olds.
Following MDA the prevalence of C. trachomatis infection declined gradually, dropping below 1% by five years (Figure 2 & Table 5).
78.Rationale: The passage does not state that the study lacked a control group. The study design focused on a single geographically defined area where MDA was implemented. The high treatment coverage and the observed decline in infection prevalence strongly suggest that MDA was effective. The conclusion that antibiotic treatment should not be used in low prevalence settings is not supported by the evidence presented.

78.Option: The observed re-infection episode in two villages shortly after mass antibiotic treatment was associated with travel to a religious festival in Senegal, suggesting that large-scale community gatherings may act as critical hubs for the reintroduction of C. trachomatis in low prevalence settings.
Evidence: In villages 1 and 3 the prevalence of infection increased at two months (Table 5). This increase was strongly associated with the travel of most village residents to a religious festival in Senegal one month after receiving treatment, which has been previously reported (OR 12.2, p<0.0001, 95% CI 4.0–44.1) [9].
78.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the re-infection in villages 1 and 3 was strongly associated with travel to a religious festival in Senegal. This directly supports the conclusion that large-scale community gatherings can act as critical hubs for reintroducing C. trachomatis in low prevalence settings. The statistical association (OR 12.2) further strengthens this inference.

78.Option: The marked reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces observed during the study suggests that health education on facial cleanliness was the primary driver of the decline in trachoma prevalence, independent of antibiotic treatment.
Evidence: There was a marked reduction in observed fly-eye contacts at the time of examination in 1 to 9 year olds from 18.0% (95% CI 14.7 to 21.5) at baseline to 1.5% (95% CI 0.5–2.5) at 5 years.

Similarly there was a large reduction in the proportion of 1 to 9 year olds with unclean faces (nasal or ocular discharge present): 38.4% (95% CI 34.1 to 42.7) at baseline and 5.0% (95% CI 3.1 to 6.8) at 5 years.
Following MDA the prevalence of C. trachomatis infection declined gradually, dropping below 1% by five years (Figure 2 & Table 5).
78.Rationale: While the passage notes a reduction in fly-eye contacts and unclean faces, it does not attribute the decline in trachoma prevalence solely to health education on facial cleanliness. The decline in C. trachomatis infection is explicitly linked to MDA. The study also implemented other components of the SAFE strategy, including surgery and environmental improvements (latrines). The evidence does not support the claim that facial cleanliness was the primary driver independent of antibiotic treatment.

78.Option: Applying the current WHO intervention algorithms in low prevalence trachoma settings may lead to unnecessary rounds of mass drug administration in uninfected communities.
Evidence: The correlation between disease and infection is not particularly strong and tends to weaken as the community prevalence drops [5], [6].

After the two-month follow-up TF%1–9 was found to exceed the 10% treatment threshold in several villages on one or more occasions (Table 4). However, in only 4/12 (33%) of these occasions were cases of chlamydial infection detected in the same village (Table 5).

Conversely, after the two-month follow-up chlamydial infection was detected in samples from several villages on one or more occasions (Table 5). However, TF%1–9 exceeded 10% on only 6/16 (37.5%) of these occasions (Table 4).
78.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the WHO's TF%1–9 threshold for initiating treatment may not accurately reflect infection status in low prevalence settings. Specifically, TF%1–9 exceeded the 10% threshold in several villages without corresponding chlamydial infection, and chlamydial infection was detected without TF%1–9 exceeding the threshold. This discrepancy suggests that relying solely on TF%1–9 could lead to unnecessary MDA in uninfected communities. The weakening correlation between disease and infection at low prevalence further supports this conclusion.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized care stems from the belief that outpatient treatment is more effective in healing BU lesions than hospital-based care.
Evidence: Decentralized treatment, for all but very severe cases of BU, still enables a woman to do chores and watch her children as well as engage in entrepreneurial activities essential for household survival.
Effective outpatient antibiotic treatment at early stages reduces wound dressings and avoids skin grafts, which are needed for large ulcerations.
79.Rationale: The passage does not state that outpatient treatment is believed to be more effective than hospital-based care. Instead, it notes that outpatient treatment is effective at early stages and allows for continued household activities. The belief in effectiveness is not cited as a reason for preference.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized BU treatment is primarily due to the greater availability of trained health staff at local health stations compared to centralized hospitals.
Evidence: Decentralized management of BU patients is well established in the Atlantic, Ouémé and Couffo regions of Benin. In these regions, most cases of mild to moderate BU (Category I and II) are treated at health stations staffed by nurses.
More serious (category II and III) cases are referred to reference hospitals.
79.Rationale: The passage does not compare the availability of trained health staff between local health stations and centralized hospitals. It only states that nurses staff the health stations and that more serious cases are referred to hospitals. There is no evidence that the preference is due to greater availability of staff at local stations.

79.Option: Households prefer decentralized care because hospital-based treatment for BU is often delayed due to long waiting lists and limited bed availability.
Evidence: The mission of peripheral centers, which are state run health stations, is to provide accessible care for simple cases of BU (category 1 and 2).
Reference centers, like the Catholic mission hospital of Zagnanado, are in charge of severe cases.
79.Rationale: The passage does not mention long waiting lists or limited bed availability as reasons for preferring decentralized care. It does not provide evidence of delays in hospital-based treatment due to these factors.

79.Option: The preference for decentralized BU treatment over hospital care is driven not only by geographical accessibility but also by the need to avoid indirect and opportunity costs, preserve social capital, and mitigate social risks such as rumors about moral impropriety.
Evidence: Decentralized care does not disrupt a household’s daily routine by removing a mother from her household.
Decentralized treatment avoids the many indirect and opportunity costs associated with hospitalization.
It allows children to stay in school while being treated, and it reduces the need to remove children from school and apprenticeships to serve as patient caretakers.
A mother does not have to worry about the quality of sibling care and foster care in her absence.
She also does not have to worry about pernicious rumors undermining her own or a daughter’s reputation.
Remaining at home during treatment is less stressful. A woman worries less about abandonment.
79.Rationale: The passage explicitly lists multiple factors driving the preference for decentralized care: avoiding indirect and opportunity costs, preserving social capital, mitigating social risks like rumors, and reducing stress. These factors are directly mentioned in the text and align with the option.

79.Option: Households prefer decentralized care because it allows for greater involvement of traditional healers in the treatment process, which strengthens cultural continuity and trust.
Evidence: The mission of peripheral centers, which are state run health stations, is to provide accessible care for simple cases of BU (category 1 and 2).
More serious (category II and III) cases are referred to reference hospitals.
79.Rationale: The passage does not mention traditional healers being involved in decentralized care. It focuses on state-run health stations and nurses. There is no evidence that the preference is due to traditional healer involvement.

80.Option: The model's conclusion that full access to antivenom is cost-saving is contingent on all snakebite victims in ASEAN countries switching from traditional to conventional treatment, which may not be feasible in remote rural areas.
Evidence: In the full access scenario, all snakebite victims who firstly sought traditional healers when access is now full are assumed to switch to conventional treatment.

However, not all victims with snakebite envenoming in ASEAN countries could access to geographically appropriate antivenoms for many reasons including inadequate supplies of antivenom, inefficient supply chain system, and inappropriate treatment seeking behavior.
80.Rationale: The model does assume that victims who initially seek traditional treatment will switch to conventional treatment in the full access scenario, but this is a modeling assumption rather than a condition for the cost-saving conclusion. The passage states that the cost-saving conclusion is robust across sensitivity analyses, including those that incorporate PTSD and increased logistics costs. The feasibility of treatment switching in remote areas is not addressed in the passage and is an external consideration not mentioned in the evidence.

80.Option: Improving access to antivenom in ASEAN countries would be cost-effective only if antivenom effectiveness exceeds a relative risk of death of 1.19 in Myanmar and 0.35 in Lao PDR.
Evidence: Threshold analyses of antivenom effectiveness and costs of antivenom treatment resulted in an ICER of 0 are shown in S4 Table. The lowest level of antivenom effectiveness, presented as a risk ratio of death in indicated victims who were not treated with antivenom compared to those treated with antivenom, was ranging from 0.35 in Lao PDR to 1.19 in Myanmar.
80.Rationale: The threshold analysis shows that even at the lowest level of antivenom effectiveness (0.35 in Lao PDR to 1.19 in Myanmar), the intervention would still be cost-effective. The option incorrectly states that effectiveness must exceed these values, when in fact the intervention remains cost-effective at these lower thresholds. The passage states that the cost-saving conclusion is robust across sensitivity analyses.

80.Option: The high burden of snakebite in ASEAN countries, including 243,000 victims and 16,000 deaths annually, underscores the urgent need for increased antivenom production to meet demand.
Evidence: Our previous study estimated that there were approximately 243,000 snakebite victims with 16,000 deaths and 950 amputations from snakebite envenoming in seven ASEAN countries, namely, Malaysia, Thailand, Indonesia, Philippines, Vietnam, Lao PDR, and Myanmar.

However, not all victims with snakebite envenoming in ASEAN countries could access to geographically appropriate antivenoms for many reasons including inadequate supplies of antivenom, inefficient supply chain system, and inappropriate treatment seeking behavior.
80.Rationale: While the passage does mention the high burden of snakebite in ASEAN countries, it also states that the issue is not solely about production but also about access, supply chain, and treatment seeking behavior. The passage emphasizes that improving access requires a multifaceted approach beyond just increasing production. Therefore, the inference that increased production is the urgent need is not directly supported by the passage.

80.Option: The inclusion of post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in the model significantly increased the incremental cost-effectiveness ratio (ICER) per DALY averted, making the intervention less cost-effective.
Evidence: Scenario analyses were performed by incorporating post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in the model as a mental disability occurring in 8% of victims who survived snakebite envenoming.
Scenario analyses found that the base-case conclusions were robust.
80.Rationale: The passage states that incorporating PTSD into the model did not significantly change the cost-effectiveness conclusions. The option incorrectly claims that it made the intervention less cost-effective, which is not supported by the evidence in the passage. The passage explicitly states that the base-case conclusions were robust across various scenario analyses, including the inclusion of PTSD.

80.Option: Improving access to snake antivenom from the current level to full access in ASEAN countries would result in cost-saving despite higher direct costs, due to substantial reductions in indirect costs from avoided productivity losses.
Evidence: Full access to antivenom had higher direct costs (2 million USD in Philippines to 50 million USD in Indonesia) but less indirect costs (-1,091 million USD in Indonesia to -28 million USD in Philippines) when compared to current access which resulted in total cost savings of 27 million USD in Philippines to 1,040 million USD in Indonesia.

In total, when compared to current access, full access to snake antivenom in ASEAN countries resulted in 9,362 deaths averted (-59%), 230,075 DALYs averted (-59%), and cost savings of 1.3 billion USD (-53%).
80.Rationale: The passage directly states that full access to antivenom results in cost savings despite higher direct costs, due to substantial reductions in indirect costs from avoided productivity losses. This is explicitly mentioned in the base-case analyses section, where the cost savings are attributed to the reduction in indirect costs. The evidence clearly supports this conclusion.

81.Option: The FP assay's ability to detect compound binding to Brugia Hsp90 is compromised by high background fluorescence from nematode extracts, which necessitates the use of complex normalization procedures in drug screening.
Evidence: The signal amplified with time, reached equilibrium at 8 h and remained stable thereafter, resulting in an excellent assay window of approximately 200 mP (Figure 1A).

To evaluate the applicability of the 96-well FP assay for HTS, the assay performance parameters S:N and Z' were determined to be 32 and 0.87, respectively (Figure 2D). For FP, a S:N value higher than 8 is recommended for reliable readings. The Z' factor is a parameter for the quality of the assay itself without test compounds. Assays with a Z' factor between 0.5 and 1.0 are considered to be reliable, robust, and suitable for HTS [32].
81.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the FP assay achieved an excellent assay window of approximately 200 mP and that the signal-to-noise ratio (S:N) was 32, which is well above the recommended threshold of 8 for reliable FP readings. The Z' factor of 0.87 also indicates a robust and reliable assay suitable for high-throughput screening. These data directly contradict the claim that the FP assay's ability is compromised by high background fluorescence requiring complex normalization procedures. The passage does not mention any issues with background fluorescence or the need for complex normalization in the context of drug screening.

81.Option: The FP assay is not suitable for identifying selective inhibitors of Brugia Hsp90 because the structural similarity between Brugia and human Hsp90 prevents meaningful discrimination between the two species.
Evidence: While GA interacted with identical affinity with both human and Brugia species, a good selectivity was noted in the PU-series, with a selectivity ratio of 2, 3.5 and 9 for PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10, respectively (Figure 4C). The 3-fold change in selectivity ratio between PU-H71 and PU-WS10, two agents of almost identical chemical structure, further confirms the sensitivity of the Hsp90 pocket to the cellular environment and suggests that it may be possible to identify molecules that specifically target the parasite Hsp90.

The FP assay developed for tumor cell Hsp90 is applicable to the parasitic nematode Brugia, as demonstrated by specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors and the ability to distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species.
81.Rationale: The passage clearly demonstrates that the FP assay can distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species, as evidenced by the selectivity ratios observed for the PU-series compounds. The data show that while GA interacts with both species with identical affinity, the PU-series compounds exhibit varying degrees of selectivity. This directly contradicts the claim that the FP assay is not suitable for identifying selective inhibitors due to structural similarity. The ability to detect differences in inhibitor selectivity between species indicates that the assay is indeed suitable for identifying selective inhibitors.

81.Option: The FP assay developed for tumor cell Hsp90 is applicable to the parasitic nematode Brugia, as demonstrated by specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors and the ability to distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species.
Evidence: The signal amplified with time, reached equilibrium at 8 h and remained stable thereafter, resulting in an excellent assay window of approximately 200 mP (Figure 1A).

To evaluate the applicability of the 96-well FP assay for HTS, the assay performance parameters S:N and Z' were determined to be 32 and 0.87, respectively (Figure 2D). For FP, a S:N value higher than 8 is recommended for reliable readings. The Z' factor is a parameter for the quality of the assay itself without test compounds. Assays with a Z' factor between 0.5 and 1.0 are considered to be reliable, robust, and suitable for HTS [32].

While GA interacted with identical affinity with both human and Brugia species, a good selectivity was noted in the PU-series, with a selectivity ratio of 2, 3.5 and 9 for PU-24FCl, PU-H71 and PU-WS10, respectively (Figure 4C). The 3-fold change in selectivity ratio between PU-H71 and PU-WS10, two agents of almost identical chemical structure, further confirms the sensitivity of the Hsp90 pocket to the cellular environment and suggests that it may be possible to identify molecules that specifically target the parasite Hsp90.

The FP assay developed for tumor cell Hsp90 is applicable to the parasitic nematode Brugia, as demonstrated by specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors and the ability to distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species.
81.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting the applicability of the FP assay to Brugia. The assay achieved an excellent signal-to-noise ratio (S:N = 32) and a high Z' factor (0.87), both of which indicate a robust and reliable assay suitable for high-throughput screening. The assay demonstrated specific and dose-dependent inhibition of cy3B-GA binding by known Hsp90 inhibitors such as GA, radicicol, and PU-series compounds. Furthermore, the assay was able to distinguish between Brugia and human Hsp90 species based on the selectivity ratios of the PU-series compounds. These findings collectively support the conclusion that the FP assay developed for tumor cell Hsp90 is indeed applicable to Brugia and can be used to identify selective inhibitors.

81.Option: The high-throughput screening capability of the FP assay is limited by the need for purified recombinant Hsp90 protein, which increases cost and complexity in drug discovery efforts for parasitic nematodes.
Evidence: The assay is suitable for high-throughput screening, does not require the production of purified recombinant protein and could be adapted to screen Hsp90 inhibitors against other parasites, where Hsp90 is known to be a valid target [24], [25].
81.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the FP assay does not require the production of purified recombinant protein, which directly contradicts the claim that the high-throughput screening capability is limited by this requirement. The ability to use worm extracts instead of purified recombinant protein reduces both cost and complexity, making the assay more feasible for drug discovery efforts. Therefore, the option is incorrect as it misrepresents the requirements and capabilities of the FP assay as described in the passage.

82.Option: The presence of TcTASV-C in extracellular vesicles from multiple T. cruzi strains suggests that this secretion pattern is a conserved virulence strategy across all parasite lineages.
Evidence: TcTASV-C was secreted in EVs in all the strains analyzed (Fig 4). Only mild differences in TcTASV-C secretion profile were detected among strains.

In the low-virulent SylvioX10 strain, TcTASV-C seemed to be poorly represented in small (V16) EVs, while in 173 strain (DTU TcI)–middle-virulence- the secretion profile of TcTASV-C was quite similar to that found in CL Brener strain (Fig 3), which is also of intermediate virulence in the murine model. In culture-derived trypomastigotes from highly virulent strains (i.e. Y, TcII and RA, TcVI) a more dynamic pattern of secretion was observed, and TcTASV-C was detected alternatively in different fractions (Fig 4 and S3 Fig).
82.Rationale: The passage states that TcTASV-C is secreted in EVs in all analyzed strains, but also notes that there are mild differences in secretion profiles among strains. The mention of 'mild differences' and the specific examples of low-virulent and highly virulent strains with distinct secretion patterns contradicts the claim that the secretion pattern is a conserved virulence strategy across all parasite lineages. The evidence shows variation rather than conservation.

82.Option: The differential expression of TcTASV-C between bloodstream and culture-derived trypomastigotes indicates that in vitro culture conditions fail to replicate the host immune pressure necessary for proper antigen expression.
Evidence: TcTASV-C was detected in 1x10^6 (or even less) bloodstream parasites (Fig 5A, upper panel, BT lanes) while it was necessary to load more than tenfold of culture-derived trypomastigotes to be weakly detected (Fig 5A, Cult lanes, upper panel). This finding was observed in all T. cruzi strains assayed, belonging to different lineages, and proved that TcTASV-C is upregulated in bloodstream forms.

Importantly, this differential expression pattern between both types of trypomastigotes was not observed with other proteins of T. cruzi, which were detected accordingly to the parasite amount loaded on the gel (Fig 5A, middle and lower panels).
82.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TcTASV-C is upregulated in bloodstream forms compared to culture-derived trypomastigotes, but does not attribute this to the absence of host immune pressure in vitro. The differential expression is presented as a biological characteristic of the protein in different life stages, not as an artifact of culture conditions. The evidence does not support the claim about in vitro culture failing to replicate host immune pressure.

82.Option: The secretion of TcTASV-C into extracellular vesicles is a parasite-driven immune evasion mechanism that helps conceal the protein from host immune surveillance.
Evidence: Pooled sera from humans, mice or rabbits chronically infected with T. cruzi failed to efficiently detect EVs antigens, although they strongly reacted with trypomastigote antigens and with 'naked' secreted proteins, both from RA and CL Brener strains (Fig 7 and S6 Fig). These findings led us to hypothesize that immunization with TcTASV-C–which is highly expressed in bloodstream trypomastigotes and also secreted- would be a good target for immunotherapy control.
The delivery of molecules in vesicles is a well known immune evasion mechanism exploited by parasites [27].
82.Rationale: The passage directly supports this inference by showing that infected hosts fail to recognize TcTASV-C in extracellular vesicles despite strong reactivity to other parasite antigens. This is explicitly linked to the hypothesis that vesicle-mediated delivery is an immune evasion strategy. The reference to established immune evasion mechanisms in parasites further strengthens this conclusion.

82.Option: The upregulation of TcTASV-C in bloodstream trypomastigotes compared to culture-derived forms suggests that the protein plays a critical role in parasite survival within the host environment.
Evidence: TcTASV-C was detected in 1x10^6 (or even less) bloodstream parasites (Fig 5A, upper panel, BT lanes) while it was necessary to load more than tenfold of culture-derived trypomastigotes to be weakly detected (Fig 5A, Cult lanes, upper panel). This finding was observed in all T. cruzi strains assayed, belonging to different lineages, and proved that TcTASV-C is upregulated in bloodstream forms.
TcTASV-C immunized animals presented a strong humoral response and a partially delayed appearance of bloodstream trypomastigotes.

Altogether these results support the idea that the broad anti-peptide reactivity of immunized mice is probably mediating the partial resistance and/or the delay in the appearance of circulating trypomastigotes in challenged mice.
82.Rationale: The evidence shows that TcTASV-C is significantly upregulated in the bloodstream form, which is the stage responsible for host transmission and systemic infection. The fact that immunization against TcTASV-C leads to delayed parasite appearance and reduced parasitemia strongly suggests that the protein plays a critical role in parasite survival within the host. The consistent upregulation across all strains further supports its biological importance in the host environment.

82.Option: The failure of chronically infected hosts to recognize TcTASV-C in extracellular vesicles suggests that the protein is degraded before reaching the immune system.
Evidence: Pooled sera from humans, mice or rabbits chronically infected with T. cruzi failed to efficiently detect EVs antigens, although they strongly reacted with trypomastigote antigens and with 'naked' secreted proteins, both from RA and CL Brener strains (Fig 7 and S6 Fig).
82.Rationale: The passage states that infected hosts failed to detect EV antigens, but this is presented as evidence of immune evasion rather than protein degradation. The text explicitly contrasts the failure to detect EV antigens with the strong reactivity to 'naked' secreted proteins, suggesting the issue is with immune recognition rather than protein integrity. There is no evidence in the passage to support the claim that the protein is degraded before reaching the immune system.

83.Option: Exposure to sand fly saliva in individuals with filarial infection leads to a shift toward a Th1-dominated immune response, enhancing protection against Leishmania.
Evidence: monocytes of SF+/MF+ individuals showed a significantly higher expression of CCL22, a type-2 chemokine
monocytes from individuals naturally exposed to sand fly bites exhibit a dampened response to stimulation with any antigen
exposure to sand fly bites also dampened the monocyte response to MF antigen and even to LPS, observed for some cytokines

84.Option: The lower seroconversion rates in Bolivia may be attributed to the use of rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) in the Sucre program, which have lower sensitivity and may have led to misclassification of true positive cases.

For Chagas Stat-Pak RDT, positive results were those samples giving two pink/purple lines, one in test area and one in control area, at reading at 15 minutes (maximum 30 minutes). Tests with no line visible in the control area were considered invalid, and these samples were retested using a new device.

84.Rationale: The passage provides detailed information about the diagnostic protocols used in the Sucre program. While RDTs were used for screening, all positive results were confirmed using conventional ELISA and HAI with recombinant ELISA as a tiebreaker. The passage also describes systematic quality control measures for RDTs, including retesting of invalid results and external QC of 10% of positive and negative samples. The average monthly isolation rates of B. pseudomallei from each layer are shown. The moisture (water) contents in the superficial layer are labeled with grey diamonds (A), and the isolation rates are shown for the indicated moisture contents (B).

In this study, the isolation rates in soils correlated with the soil moisture content (Fig 2B) (r2 = 0.8828). In Taiwan, typhoons that carry heavy rainfalls and result in floods usually occur from July to October (typhoon season).

The monthly PCR-positive rates for aerosols collected from the core region significantly increased from June to November compared with the rates estimated from the surrounding areas (t-test, p<0.05). The molecular type PFGE Ia (ST58) linked the soil isolates in the superficial layer to aerosols and human samples.

The distinct ST types (Taiwanese ST types, shown as green circles; Thai ST types, shown as red circles or localized at the region in pink; Australian ST types, localized at the region in blue) that were identified using eBurst (v3) analysis are shown. ST1354 (singleton) is a novel ST type, shown on the left.
86.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that B. pseudomallei was not found in urban grounds or urban green areas. Instead, the pathogen was isolated from cropped fields and wetted but non-cropped fields in the northwestern outskirts of the core region. The presence of B. pseudomallei in aerosols is correlated with northwesterly winds and the isolation rates from these northwestern cropped fields, not with urban green areas. The option is directly contradicted by the evidence presented in the passage.

87.Option: The use of DDT in swamps and sewers during the 1940s and 1950s was a major contributor to the decline of Ae. aegypti in Brisbane, as it effectively eliminated larval habitats and adult populations across the city.
Evidence: The passage does not mention the use of DDT or any chemical insecticides in swamps and sewers during the 1940s and 1950s in Brisbane.
87.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence of DDT use in Brisbane during the specified period. The focus of the historical data is on regulatory enforcement, rainwater tank compliance, and surveillance, not on chemical interventions. Therefore, this option cannot be derived from the passage.

87.Option: The elimination of Aedes aegypti from Brisbane was likely driven primarily by the removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks as larval habitat, supported by sustained regulatory enforcement and public compliance.
Evidence: The passage states that rainwater tanks were a key larval habitat for Ae. aegypti and that regulations were introduced in 1911 to prevent mosquitoes from exploiting these tanks.

Compliance with anti-mosquito regulations typically involved the resident sealing or removing rainwater tanks that were at risk of breeding mosquitoes and removing or treating other potential containers.

The proportion of surveys resulting in a notice for a breach in regulations dropped after 1954, and compliance with notices was high during the 1950s and 1960s (the period when Ae. aegypti disappeared).
The estimated number of non-compliant tanks declined steadily from 1911 to 1971, with a significant drop after 1955.
The probability of observing Ae. aegypti after 1957 was calculated as P = 0.042, below the threshold of 0.05, suggesting the population does not exist.
87.Rationale: The passage provides extensive evidence that the elimination of Ae. aegypti in Brisbane was driven by the removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks as larval habitat, supported by regulatory enforcement and public compliance. The data show a decline in non-compliant tanks, increased compliance with regulations, and a corresponding decline in Ae. aegypti sightings, leading to the conclusion that the species was likely eliminated.

87.Option: The improvement of living standards and urban design after the Second World War, including the replacement of backyards with recreational spaces, was the main reason for the disappearance of Ae. aegypti from Brisbane.
Evidence: The passage does not mention improvements in living standards or urban design, nor does it reference the replacement of backyards with recreational spaces as a factor in the disappearance of Ae. aegypti.
The focus is on regulatory enforcement, rainwater tank compliance, and surveillance, not on changes in urban design or living standards.
87.Rationale: The passage does not provide any evidence to support the claim that improvements in living standards or urban design were the main reasons for the disappearance of Ae. aegypti from Brisbane. Therefore, this option cannot be derived from the passage.

87.Option: The decline in dengue transmission after 1943 was primarily due to the natural reduction in mosquito populations caused by climatic changes, rather than public health interventions.
Evidence: The passage states that the decline in dengue transmission after 1943 was associated with increased regulatory enforcement and surveillance efforts, particularly during the 1950s and 1960s.
The probability of observing Ae. aegypti after 1957 was calculated as P = 0.042, below the threshold of 0.05, suggesting the population does not exist.
The estimated number of non-compliant tanks declined steadily from 1911 to 1971, with a significant drop after 1955.
87.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that public health interventions, including regulatory enforcement and the removal of non-compliant rainwater tanks, were responsible for the decline in dengue transmission after 1943, not natural climatic changes. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

87.Option: The introduction of a reliable reticulated water supply in 1954 coincided with the disappearance of Ae. aegypti from Brisbane, suggesting that the replacement of rainwater tanks with piped water was the primary factor in the species' elimination.
Evidence: The passage states that the introduction of a large water reservoir to supply Brisbane in 1954 led to a consistent decline in the mean number of rainwater tanks per dwelling below 0.50 tanks.
The estimated number of non-compliant tanks declined steadily from 1911 to 1971, with a significant drop after 1955.
The probability of observing Ae. aegypti after 1957 was calculated as P = 0.042, below the threshold of 0.05, suggesting the population does not exist.
87.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the introduction of a reliable reticulated water supply in 1954 led to a significant reduction in the number of rainwater tanks, which in turn reduced the availability of larval habitats for Ae. aegypti. This reduction in larval habitats, combined with regulatory enforcement, likely contributed to the species' elimination. Therefore, this option is correct.

88.Option: The use of itraconazole in PCM patients may have protected them from severe SARS-CoV-2 infection by reducing viral replication rates, explaining the absence of post-COVID-19 PCM cases.
Evidence: The patient was treated with itraconazole and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole for 35 months due to poor adherence.
Although only two patients were under treatment for PCM when they had COVID-19, there was no need to change the therapeutic regimen for PCM in these cases.
88.Rationale: The passage mentions that one patient was treated with itraconazole, but this was for a 35-month period due to poor adherence. The passage also states that there was no need to change the therapeutic regimen for PCM in the two patients who had COVID-19 while under treatment. There is no evidence in the passage to suggest that itraconazole protected against SARS-CoV-2 infection or reduced viral replication rates. The absence of post-COVID-19 PCM cases is not explained by itraconazole use in the passage.

88.Option: The high incidence of acute PCM in urban areas of Rio de Janeiro may be directly linked to increased susceptibility to SARS-CoV-2 infection due to immune system alterations in acute PCM patients.
Evidence: Rio de Janeiro state is an important endemic region for this mycosis, and there has recently been an increase in the incidence of acute PCM forms in urban areas.
acute PCM rarely affects these organs.
88.Rationale: The passage mentions an increase in acute PCM in urban areas of Rio de Janeiro, but it does not establish a direct link between this increase and increased susceptibility to SARS-CoV-2 infection. The passage also states that acute PCM rarely affects the lungs and upper airways, which are the organs involved in SARS-CoV-2 infection. There is no evidence in the passage to suggest that immune system alterations in acute PCM patients lead to increased susceptibility to SARS-CoV-2 infection.

88.Option: Co-infection with SARS-CoV-2 and Paracoccidioides manifests in mild to lethal outcomes with increased odds of poor outcomes when the lungs are compromised due to PCM.
Evidence: Three patients presented with mild symptoms of COVID-19, including low-grade fever, headache, and other common cold symptoms (cough, sneezing and nasal discharge). The other three patients required hospitalization due to hypoxia.

One patient had acute PCM under treatment with pulmonary involvement and we previously reported this case [7]. ... he subsequently developed a dilated cardiomyopathy as a complication of COVID-19 after discharge, but recovered and was lost follow-up after 17 months of PCM treatment.

The other patient with severe COVID-19 had chronic PCM under post-therapeutic follow-up with severe pulmonary fibrosis, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and, despite treatment in the ICU with mechanical ventilation, corticosteroids, and antibiotics, died four days after admission due to rapid decompensation due to complications of COVID-19.
88.Rationale: The passage describes a range of outcomes for co-infection with SARS-CoV-2 and Paracoccidioides, from mild symptoms to lethal outcomes. The evidence shows that patients with compromised lungs due to PCM (either acute or chronic) had worse outcomes. The patient with acute PCM and pulmonary involvement developed a complication of COVID-19 (dilated cardiomyopathy), and the patient with chronic PCM and severe pulmonary fibrosis/COPD died from complications of COVID-19. This supports the inference that co-infection can lead to mild to lethal outcomes, with increased odds of poor outcomes when the lungs are compromised due to PCM.

88.Option: The absence of new case reports of PCM and COVID-19 co-infection is primarily due to the high rate of vaccination among PCM patients, which reduced the risk of SARS-CoV-2 transmission.
Evidence: The confounders of overlapping clinical manifestations may explain in part the absence of new case reports of PCM and COVID-19 co-infection.
88.Rationale: The passage attributes the absence of new case reports of PCM and COVID-19 co-infection to the confounders of overlapping clinical manifestations, not to high vaccination rates among PCM patients. There is no mention of vaccination rates or their impact on SARS-CoV-2 transmission in the passage.

88.Option: The immune dysregulation and inflammation occurring in COVID-19 likely leads to a higher risk of symptomatic disease and reactivation of endemic mycoses in individuals with previously latent infections.
Evidence: The confounders of overlapping clinical manifestations may explain in part the absence of new case reports of PCM and COVID-19 co-infection.
The difficulty of diagnosing PCM during hospitalization through the COVID-19 pandemic has also been reported, resulting in delayed diagnosis of PCM.
88.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that immune dysregulation and inflammation in COVID-19 lead to a higher risk of symptomatic disease or reactivation of endemic mycoses. The passage mentions the difficulty of diagnosing PCM during the pandemic and the absence of new case reports, but these are attributed to overlapping clinical manifestations and diagnostic challenges, not to immune dysregulation or reactivation of latent infections.

89.Option: Stigma associated with ZCL is primarily rooted in the belief that the disease is contagious and poses a public health threat to others.
Evidence: The respondents mentioned the human responsibility for the environmental changes.
The majority considered that ZCL is not hereditary (80%). It was not related to the quality of food (92%), or stress in everyday life (78%).
73% linked the environment (stagnant water) and physical activity (working alongside animals) with the incidence of ZCL.
89.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that stigma is rooted in the belief that ZCL is contagious or a public health threat. Instead, it indicates that respondents associate ZCL with environmental factors and physical activity, not contagion. The belief that ZCL is not hereditary or related to food or stress further supports that contagion is not a primary concern for stigma.

89.Option: The number of facial and body scars is the primary determinant of the level of stigma experienced by ZCL patients, regardless of their location or perception of the disease.
Evidence: There is a moderate correlation and just around the significant level between the total number of body scars and 'rejection experiences' (r = 0.31, p<0.05), and between the number of facial scars and the scores on 'Anticipation and avoidance of stress' (r = 0.29, p = 0.06).

It is important to note that this correlation between the number of scars and stigma indices was at the same threshold of significance when checked against age-related variations. Hence, the number of body scars had a strong link to experience with stigma, regardless of a patient’s age.
89.Rationale: While the passage notes a correlation between the number of scars and stigma, it also emphasizes that this correlation is not the primary determinant. The study highlights that stigma is more strongly influenced by psychological and social factors, such as anticipated rejection and emotional representations, rather than just the number of scars. The correlation is significant but not the primary factor.

89.Option: The nature of stigma experienced by ZCL patients is primarily driven by anticipated rejection rather than actual experiences of rejection from society.
Evidence: The descriptive data focused more on anticipation of a negative reaction from others and the efforts made to display anticipatory avoidance behaviour meant to deny such reactions, than on convictions from the presence of negative experiences that patients have had through social interactions on the state of their skin (Table 4).

Rejection experiences and the 'anticipation and avoidance of stress' were respectively, significantly and negatively correlated with age (r = -0.33, p<0.05 and r = -0.31, p<0.05). The younger the subject, the higher the number of experiences tied to stigma; and the older the subject, the more such feelings are dismissed.

The results showed that anticipation and avoidance of stress is strongly correlated with the scores on 'Consequences', 'Coherence' and 'Emotional representation.' These results suggest that the more a person looks at the world anticipating segregation and rejection, the more they perceive ZCL as a disease with harmful effects on them, the more they see it as a mysterious and incomprehensible illness, and the more they talk about suffering and emotional difficulties.
89.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the descriptive data focused more on the anticipation of negative reactions and anticipatory avoidance behavior rather than actual experiences of rejection. The correlation analysis also shows that younger subjects experience more stigma due to anticipation, and the results indicate a strong link between anticipation and emotional responses. This directly supports the conclusion that the stigma is primarily driven by anticipated rejection.

89.Option: The most significant factor influencing quality of life among ZCL patients is the physical severity of their skin lesions, particularly facial scarring.
Evidence: Quality of life scores were not significantly correlated with either age, education level, time of illness, or the number of facial or body scars (Table 5).

On the other hand, quality of life scores had interesting correlations with PLSI scores on stigma (Figs 3 and 4). The quality of social life score was significantly and negatively correlated to the 'Anticipation and avoidance of stress' score (r = -0.36, p<0.05) suggesting that the more a person anticipates segregation and psychosocial stress, the lower their quality of life was seen to be.
The quality of knowledge seems therefore to be tied closely to the quality of social life (Table 6).
89.Rationale: The passage clearly states that quality of life scores were not significantly correlated with the physical severity of skin lesions or the number of scars. Instead, the study found that quality of life is more closely related to psychosocial factors such as stigma, anticipation of rejection, and knowledge about the disease. This indicates that physical severity is not the most significant factor.

90.Option: The use of serological markers in population screening studies in Mazán could significantly improve the detection of asymptomatic infections compared to light microscopy, thereby enhancing the effectiveness of active case detection programs.
Evidence: Plasmodium vivax causes the majority of malaria cases in Peru. The detection of cases is based on light microscopy (LM) diagnosis; this may substantially underestimate prevalence as greater than 50% of infections that are detectable by qPCR may be missed by microscopy [6]. Being based on the detection of blood-stage parasites, LM and qPCR do not detect individuals with only liver-stage hypnozoite infections. Therefore, detecting transmission changes using LM or qPCR data may overlook P. vivax ultra-low-density infections [11] and hypnozoite carriers, thus underestimating transmission levels.

New uses of malaria serological tools have been proposed to inform the impact of such control measures [12]. Ideally, the detection of short-lived antibody responses in a given population’s inhabitants will indicate that recent exposure has happened. Recently, new serological markers of exposure to P. vivax have been developed and validated for their use in low transmission settings [13]. These serological markers of exposure were able to detect recent exposure in participants from longitudinal cohorts of road and riverine communities with moderate-to-high transmission of the Peruvian Amazon, although performance was notably lower than in low endemic settings [14].

Mazán and Iquitos are located within the Maynas province, in the department of Loreto. The ICEMR study surveyed seven communities from the Mazán district: Gaminatacocha (GC), Libertad (LI), Primero de Enero (PE), Puerto Alegre (PA), Salvador (SA), Urco Miraño (UM) and Lago Yuracyacu (LY). The CAM study surveyed three communities from Iquitos: Rumococha (RC), Santo Tomás (ST) and Quistococha (QC). Each circle represents a household location. Map generated with QGIS 2.16 (QGIS Development Team, 2016. QGIS Geographic Information System. Open Source Geospatial Foundation Project) based on public geographic data extracted from OpenStreetMap contributors (www.openstreetmap.org) under Open Data Commons Open Database License (ODbL) 1.0 (http://openstreetmap.org/copyright).

P. vivax prevalence by LM in Iquitos was not significantly different from Mazán (1.8%, 16/880, vs 1.3%, 13/1024, p = 0.329). Although P. vivax prevalence by qPCR was higher in Iquitos than in Mazán (5.3%, 47/880, vs 2.7%, 28/1024, p = 0.005); Mazán (56.5%, 579/1024) showed higher seroprevalence than Iquitos (38.2%, 336/880, p < 0.001) (Table2). Only 14% (47/336) and 5% (28/579) of seropositive individuals in Iquitos and Mazán respectively had concurrent, qPCR-detectable infections.
90.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this conclusion. First, it establishes that P. vivax is the primary malaria-causing parasite in Peru and that light microscopy (LM) underestimates prevalence by more than 50% compared to qPCR. Second, it explains that serological markers can detect recent exposure through antibody responses, which is particularly valuable in low transmission settings. Third, the study in Mazán (a riverine community) showed significantly higher seroprevalence (56.5%) compared to qPCR-detectable infections (2.7%), indicating that serology detects many asymptomatic or low-density infections missed by traditional methods. This directly supports the inference that serological markers could improve detection of asymptomatic infections in Mazán, enhancing active case detection programs.

90.Option: The higher seroprevalence in riverine communities of Mazán compared to peri-urban Iquitos indicates that serological markers are more sensitive than molecular diagnostics in detecting transmission in remote, hard-to-reach areas.
Evidence: Mazán and Iquitos are located within the Maynas province, in the department of Loreto. The ICEMR study surveyed seven communities from the Mazán district: Gaminatacocha (GC), Libertad (LI), Primero de Enero (PE), Puerto Alegre (PA), Salvador (SA), Urco Miraño (UM) and Lago Yuracyacu (LY). The CAM study surveyed three communities from Iquitos: Rumococha (RC), Santo Tomás (ST) and Quistococha (QC). Each circle represents a household location. Map generated with QGIS 2.16 (QGIS Development Team, 2016. QGIS Geographic Information System. Open Source Geospatial Foundation Project) based on public geographic data extracted from OpenStreetMap contributors (www.openstreetmap.org) under Open Data Commons Open Database License (ODbL) 1.0 (http://openstreetmap.org/copyright).

P. vivax prevalence by LM in Iquitos was not significantly different from Mazán (1.8%, 16/880, vs 1.3%, 13/1024, p = 0.329). Although P. vivax prevalence by qPCR was higher in Iquitos than in Mazán (5.3%, 47/880, vs 2.7%, 28/1024, p = 0.005); Mazán (56.5%, 579/1024) showed higher seroprevalence than Iquitos (38.2%, 336/880, p < 0.001) (Table2). Only 14% (47/336) and 5% (28/579) of seropositive individuals in Iquitos and Mazán respectively had concurrent, qPCR-detectable infections.
90.Rationale: While the passage does show higher seroprevalence in Mazán compared to Iquitos, it does not make any claims about the sensitivity of serological markers specifically in remote or hard-to-reach areas. The difference in seroprevalence could be due to actual differences in transmission intensity rather than differences in diagnostic sensitivity. The passage also notes that serological markers performed notably lower in moderate-to-high transmission settings like the Peruvian Amazon compared to low endemic settings. This option misinterprets the data by attributing the higher seroprevalence in Mazán to the sensitivity of the serological method rather than the underlying transmission dynamics.

90.Option: Because seropositivity is strongly associated with outdoor occupation in Mazán, serological markers should be used to target interventions specifically toward individuals engaged in forest-related work.
Evidence: In Mazán, the risk of being seropositive to P. vivax increased with increasing age (adjusted odds ratio, aOR5-14.9 years = 3.27; 95% CI 1.69–6.31; p < 0.001; aOR15-29 years = 13.68; 95% CI 5.78–32.38; p < 0.001; aOR30-49.9 years = 20.58; 95% CI 8.18–51.79; p < 0.001; aOR≥50 years = 28.34; 95% CI 11.05–72.68; p < 0.001), being male (aOR = 1.72; 95% CI 1.17–2.51; p = 0.01) and working outside (aOR = 2.51; 95% CI 1.43–4.41; p < 0.001). Having domestic animals was associated with a low risk of being seropositive (aOR = 0.59; 95% CI 0.35–0. 98; p = 0.04). There was no association with fever symptom and seropositivity, as well with the travel history in the last month and the type of house (S4 Table). The high ICC value for community, in contrast to households, indicated that seropositivity of participants was mostly affected by community factors or that there was homogeneity within communities, e.g., closeness to water bodies. This possibly indicates a more uniform and stable local transmission pattern (S5 Table).

Mazán is a district of Maynas province located 55–60 km northeast of Iquitos. It is comprised of many small communities, and the community of Mazán is surrounded by the Mazán and the Napo Rivers (3.503° S, 73.094° W). The only access is by speedboat (1 hour from Iquitos). Gamitanacocha (GC), Libertad (LI), Primero de Enero (PE) and Puerto Alegre (PA) are located on the Mazán River. Salvador (SA), Lago Yuracyacu (LY) and Urco Miraño (UM) are located on the Napo River. The main economic activities are farming, timber extraction and fishing [16].
90.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that seropositivity in Mazán is strongly associated with outdoor occupation (aOR = 2.51; 95% CI 1.43–4.41; p < 0.001). It also describes the primary economic activities in Mazán as farming, timber extraction, and fishing, which are all forest-related occupations. This directly supports the inference that serological markers could be used to identify individuals engaged in these high-risk occupations for targeted interventions. The statistical association between outdoor work and seropositivity provides a clear epidemiological basis for this application of serological data.

90.Option: Serological markers are most effective in low transmission settings because they can distinguish between recent and past infections with high accuracy, making them ideal for monitoring the impact of elimination programs.
Evidence: New uses of malaria serological tools have been proposed to inform the impact of such control measures [12]. Ideally, the detection of short-lived antibody responses in a given population’s inhabitants will indicate that recent exposure has happened. Recently, new serological markers of exposure to P. vivax have been developed and validated for their use in low transmission settings [13]. These serological markers of exposure were able to detect recent exposure in participants from longitudinal cohorts of road and riverine communities with moderate-to-high transmission of the Peruvian Amazon, although performance was notably lower than in low endemic settings [14].
90.Rationale: The passage states that serological markers were developed for low transmission settings but performed notably lower in moderate-to-high transmission settings like the Peruvian Amazon. However, it does not claim that they are 'most effective' in low transmission settings or that they can 'distinguish between recent and past infections with high accuracy.' In fact, the passage notes that the markers were able to detect recent exposure in moderate-to-high transmission settings, albeit with lower performance. The option makes an absolute claim about effectiveness in low transmission settings that is not supported by the passage, which only mentions that the markers were developed for such settings.

90.Option: In areas where transmission has shifted from moderate-to-high to low levels, serological markers of recent exposure are best suited to detect recent transmission in young people, as high antibody levels in this group are more likely to reflect recent infection rather than cumulative lifetime exposure.
Evidence: Seropositive rate increase with age in both study sites but was more pronounced in Mazán. Antibody levels and seropositivity rate increased with age in both study sites (S3 Fig and S2 and S3 Tables). The proportion of seropositive individuals was significantly higher in participants from Mazán than Iquitos in all age groups (age group < 15: 1.72 times higher; age group 15–29.9: 1.92 higher; age group 30–49.9: 1.54 higher; age group 50+: 1.39 higher; p mean < 0.001) (S3 Fig). There was a notable increase in the proportion seropositive people aged > 15 years in both study sites (S3 Fig). This difference was more pronounced in Mazán, where the SEM seropositivity rate in the age group 15–29.9 (0.69, 95% CI 0.60–0.76) was two times higher than in the age group 0–15 (0.31, 95% CI 0.27–0.35) (p = 0.006) (S3 Fig). Antibody titers to individual SEM also showed an age-trend in both sites for all antigens considered (S4 Fig).

In the most severe cases, a person may require: - Additional oxygen, - IV fluids if they can't drink enough to stay hydrated, or - Intubation (have a breathing tube inserted through the mouth and down to the airway) with mechanical ventilation (a machine to help a person breathe). In most of these cases, hospitalization lasts only a few days.

Plasmodium vivax causes the majority of malaria cases in Peru. The detection of cases is based on light microscopy (LM) diagnosis; this may substantially underestimate prevalence as greater than 50% of infections that are detectable by qPCR may be missed by microscopy [6]. Being based on the detection of blood-stage parasites, LM and qPCR do not detect individuals with only liver-stage hypnozoite infections. Therefore, detecting transmission changes using LM or qPCR data may overlook P. vivax ultra-low-density infections [11] and hypnozoite carriers, thus underestimating transmission levels.

New uses of malaria serological tools have been proposed to inform the impact of such control measures [12]. Ideally, the detection of short-lived antibody responses in a given population’s inhabitants will indicate that recent exposure has happened. Recently, new serological markers of exposure to P. vivax have been developed and validated for their use in low transmission settings [13]. These serological markers of exposure were able to detect recent exposure in participants from longitudinal cohorts of road and riverine communities with moderate-to-high transmission of the Peruvian Amazon, although performance was notably lower than in low endemic settings [14].
90.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that antibody levels increase with age, indicating cumulative exposure over time. In young people, high antibody levels are more likely to reflect recent infection rather than long-term exposure. This is particularly relevant in areas where transmission has decreased from moderate-to-high to low levels, as serological markers can help detect recent transmission events in younger age groups. The passage also notes that serological markers are designed to detect short-lived antibody responses, which aligns with the idea that they are useful for identifying recent infections in populations where transmission is declining.

92.Option: Continuity of patient care could be supported by funding a specialist RHD nurse who is capable of answering clinical questions.
Evidence: Most participants were aware that Pacific people were at higher risk of ARF but lacked confidence in the Victorian healthcare system’s capacity to detect cases (except at RCH), and facilitate ongoing case management.

A lack of coordination between treating physicians and restrictions around sharing medical records were identified as barriers to patients transitioning from paediatric to adult care systems without being lost to follow up, and as barriers to primary and tertiary healthcare providers liaising effectively.

Several interviewed clinicians suggested that ensuring patients’ GPs were highly engaged in their treatment. They also postulated that continuing to involve family members in the patient’s care as they transitioned into the adult care system could reduce loss to follow up, and improve the quality of care.
92.Rationale: While the passage mentions the need for better coordination and family involvement, it does not specifically mention the role of a specialist RHD nurse. The option is plausible but not directly supported by the evidence in the passage.

92.Option: Clinicians involving the family in patient care even once the patient reaches adulthood may be an acceptable way to support ongoing treatment and monitoring.
Evidence: Caregivers described their extended family providing support to manage their child’s health.

Older patients and caregivers discussed patients taking on more responsibility for their treatment as they approached adulthood. Such responsibilities included organising and attending appointments alone, paying for BPG and having conversations with clinicians. Despite this, caregivers said they would always be there to support their child to receive the treatment they need.

For us, with our community and being an Islander, that age [18 years] means nothing. That support will always be there. Either me or my sister will continue …to take him to his appointments [to have BPG injected].
92.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that family support is crucial and that caregivers continue to support their children even after they reach adulthood. This directly supports the option of involving family in patient care as an acceptable way to support ongoing treatment and monitoring.

92.Option: Introducing a patient register and a specialist RHD nurse to support best practice patient management may help to improve the continuity of care for Pacific patients with ARF/RHD in Victoria.
Evidence: In Victoria, ARF and RHD were made notifiable to the state Department of Health on 31 July 2023. There is no centralised RHD register in Victoria to provide a straightforward means of monitoring cases’ BPG uptake.

A lack of coordination between treating physicians and restrictions around sharing medical records were identified as barriers to patients transitioning from paediatric to adult care systems without being lost to follow up, and as barriers to primary and tertiary healthcare providers liaising effectively.

Several interviewed clinicians suggested that ensuring patients’ GPs were highly engaged in their treatment. They also postulated that continuing to involve family members in the patient’s care as they transitioned into the adult care system could reduce loss to follow up, and improve the quality of care.
92.Rationale: The passage highlights the absence of a centralised RHD register in Victoria and the need for better coordination between healthcare providers. Introducing a patient register and a specialist RHD nurse aligns with the recommendations from clinicians and addresses the identified barriers to continuity of care.

92.Option: A well-resourced register would enable patients to maintain their continuity of care when moving between healthcare providers, and routinely collected data could facilitate future assessments of the effectiveness of BPG delivery.
Evidence: In Victoria, ARF and RHD were made notifiable to the state Department of Health on 31 July 2023. There is no centralised RHD register in Victoria to provide a straightforward means of monitoring cases’ BPG uptake.

A lack of coordination between treating physicians and restrictions around sharing medical records were identified as barriers to patients transitioning from paediatric to adult care systems without being lost to follow up, and as barriers to primary and tertiary healthcare providers liaising effectively.
92.Rationale: The passage explicitly mentions the lack of a centralised RHD register in Victoria and the resulting challenges in monitoring BPG uptake and coordinating care. A well-resourced register would directly address these issues by enabling continuity of care and facilitating data collection for assessing BPG effectiveness.

93.Option: The emergence of streptomycin resistance in S19960127 is likely due to the presence of a novel plasmid, pS96127, which was found in both resistant and susceptible strains.
Evidence: The whole genome of Y. pestis S19960127 (sequence accession number CP045636) consists of a 4.63-megabase chromosome and four plasmids, besides three common virulence plasmid pCD1/pYV, pPCP1/pPst, pMT1/ Fra [24,25], a novel 33.9-kb plasmid (named pS96127) in the Y. pestis S19960127 genome was discovered. The novel plasmid pS96127 shared 99.95% identity and 99.98 coverage with the pTP33 plasmid in Y. pestis strain I-2638 (KT020860.1). Apart from S19960127, another 14 strains sensitive to streptomycin isolated in Shannan Prefecture also carried the pS96127 plasmid.
93.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the novel plasmid pS96127 was found in both the resistant strain S19960127 and 14 other susceptible strains. Since the resistant strain and susceptible strains share the same plasmid but differ in streptomycin resistance, the presence of pS96127 cannot explain the resistance. The study also found that the resistance was due to a mutation in the rpsL gene, not the plasmid. Therefore, the plasmid is not the cause of resistance.
