116.Option: Blocking IL-4Rα signaling during chronic schistosomiasis leads to a compensatory increase in IL-13 production, which maintains fibroproliferative pathology despite reduced IL-4 signaling.
Evidence: Innate type 2 immune effectors i.e. eosinophils [34,35], ILC2 [36] and macrophages [37] have been positively linked to liver fibrosis, the pathophysiological process that drives the host morbidity during chronic schistosomiasis.

The analysis of the MLN cells of S. mansoni-infected iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 mice for these cell types by flow cytometry (S7D Fig–S7G Fig) revealed that the pro-fibrotic innate effectors i.e. eosinophils (Fig 7P, S8A Fig and S8B Fig), ILC2 (Fig 7Q and S8C Fig) and macrophages (S8D Fig) were significantly diminished.

Taken together, these results suggest that knocking down IL-4Rα at the chronic phase of experimental schistosomiasis considerably skews the host immune response away from the type 2 arm of the immune response, fosters a qualitatively more regulatory, anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic profile with no deleterious effect on host survival.
116.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence of a compensatory increase in IL-13 production following IL-4Rα knockdown. In fact, the study found that IL-4Rα knockdown in chronically infected mice led to reduced production of both IL-4 and IL-13 (as shown in Fig 7B and 7C). The results indicate a decrease in type 2 immune responses rather than a compensatory increase in IL-13. The observed amelioration of fibroproliferative pathology is attributed to the reduction in type 2 responses, not to a compensatory mechanism. This option is therefore not supported by the evidence presented in the passage.

116.Option: The protective role of IL-4Rα signaling during acute schistosomiasis implies that its removal in the chronic phase would also compromise host survival, despite reduced tissue pathology.
Evidence: S. mansoni-infected mice following IL-4Rα knockdown at 16 weeks post-infection, i.e. iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 mice (Fig 6A) did not result in any weight loss (Fig 6B) or mortality (Fig 6C), for up to 24 weeks post infection.

Taken together, these results suggest that knocking down IL-4Rα at the chronic phase of experimental schistosomiasis considerably skews the host immune response away from the type 2 arm of the immune response, fosters a qualitatively more regulatory, anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic profile with no deleterious effect on host survival.
116.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that IL-4Rα knockdown during the chronic phase of schistosomiasis did not result in weight loss or mortality, indicating that host survival was not compromised. This directly contradicts the claim in the option that removal of IL-4Rα in the chronic phase would compromise host survival. The study's findings show that IL-4Rα signaling is not required for host survival during the chronic phase, despite its protective role during the acute phase. This option is therefore not supported by the evidence presented in the passage.

116.Option: Targeting IL-4Rα mediated signaling during chronic schistosomiasis ameliorates fibroproliferative pathology without impairing animal viability, suggesting a detrimental role for this pathway in chronic disease.
Evidence: S. mansoni-infected mice following IL-4Rα knockdown at 16 weeks post-infection, i.e. iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 mice (Fig 6A) did not result in any weight loss (Fig 6B) or mortality (Fig 6C), for up to 24 weeks post infection.
IL-4Rα knockdown considerably reduced liver (Fig 6E) and spleen (Fig 6F) enlargement in chronically infected mice.
Moreover, IL-4Rα knockdown considerably reduced granuloma size (Fig 6H and 6I) and collagen levels (Fig 6J and 6K) in the livers of chronically infected mice.

Taken together, these results suggest that knocking down IL-4Rα at the chronic phase of experimental schistosomiasis considerably skews the host immune response away from the type 2 arm of the immune response, fosters a qualitatively more regulatory, anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic profile with no deleterious effect on host survival.
116.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that IL-4Rα knockdown during the chronic phase of schistosomiasis ameliorates fibroproliferative pathology (reduced liver and spleen enlargement, granuloma size, and collagen levels) without impairing animal viability (no weight loss or mortality). This supports the inference that IL-4Rα signaling plays a detrimental role in chronic disease by promoting fibroproliferative pathology. The study's findings are consistent with the option's claim that targeting this pathway during chronic schistosomiasis is beneficial. This option is therefore supported by the evidence presented in the passage.

116.Option: IL-4Rα mediated signaling is critical for the development of immune hyporesponsiveness after chronic exposure of host immune cells to schistosomal antigens, which may contribute to the regulation of chronic disease progression.
Evidence: The analysis of the MLN cells of S. mansoni-infected iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 mice for these cell types by flow cytometry (S7D Fig–S7G Fig) revealed that the pro-fibrotic innate effectors i.e. eosinophils (Fig 7P, S8A Fig and S8B Fig), ILC2 (Fig 7Q and S8C Fig) and macrophages (S8D Fig) were significantly diminished.

The frequencies of Foxp3+ regulatory T cell responses were increased in the MLNs of S. mansoni-infected iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 mice (Fig 7G and 7H), when compared with S. mansoni-infected IL-4Rα-/lox control mice.

The analysis of the MLN cells of S. mansoni-infected iCre-/+IL-4Rα-/lox Tam16 mice for these cell types by flow cytometry (S7D Fig–S7G Fig) revealed that the pro-fibrotic innate effectors i.e. eosinophils (Fig 7P, S8A Fig and S8B Fig), ILC2 (Fig 7Q and S8C Fig) and macrophages (S8D Fig) were significantly diminished.

Taken together, these results suggest that knocking down IL-4Rα at the chronic phase of experimental schistosomiasis considerably skews the host immune response away from the type 2 arm of the immune response, fosters a qualitatively more regulatory, anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic profile with no deleterious effect on host survival.
116.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that IL-4Rα signaling is critical for the development of immune hyporesponsiveness. In fact, the study found that IL-4Rα knockdown led to an increase in regulatory T cell frequencies and a shift towards a more regulatory, anti-inflammatory profile. This suggests that IL-4Rα signaling may actually inhibit the development of immune hyporesponsiveness rather than being critical for it. The option's claim is therefore not supported by the evidence presented in the passage.

117.Option: The restriction patterns observed in the PCR-RFLP assay are consistent across all T. cruzi strains, including those from hybrid lineages, due to the stable inheritance of polymorphic restriction sites over evolutionary time.
Evidence: The two hybrid lineages (TcV and TcVI) contain two of these sites (see Table 1), both in heterozygosity, therefore we expected a pattern that is the logical addition of the patterns of lineages TcII and TcIII.
In the case of these DTUs, the banding pattern is essentially the sum of the patterns of DTUs TcII and TcIII, which agrees with the currently favored hybridization hypothesis [18].
117.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that hybrid lineages TcV and TcVI do not have consistent restriction patterns but instead show a pattern that is the 'logical addition' of their parental lineages TcII and TcIII. This contradicts the claim that restriction patterns are 'consistent across all T. cruzi strains, including hybrids.' The hybrid lineages show patterns that are combinations of their parental lineages, not stable inherited patterns.

117.Option: The PCR-RFLP assay is highly sensitive and can detect low-level contamination in parasite stocks due to its ability to amplify repetitive genomic regions, making it ideal for routine screening in clinical settings.
Evidence: The TcSC5D gene, present as a single copy per haploid genome, and mapped to chromosome 22 [36], showed the highest density of fixed differences in this dataset (11.37 SNPs per 100 bp, Cosentino RO, and Agüero F, manuscript in preparation).
117.Rationale: The passage states that the TcSC5D gene is present as a single copy per haploid genome, not as a repetitive region. The option's claim about amplifying repetitive genomic regions is therefore incorrect. Additionally, the passage does not mention sensitivity to low-level contamination or routine clinical screening as a feature of the PCR-RFLP assay.

117.Option: The proposed assay can be used to resolve all T. cruzi lineages, including TcV and TcVI, with high accuracy using only a single PCR-RFLP step without the need for additional sequencing or validation.
Evidence: These patterns are unique for all DTUs, except for DTUs TcV and TcVI, that cannot be differentiated.
In the case of these DTUs, the banding pattern is essentially the sum of the patterns of DTUs TcII and TcIII, which agrees with the currently favored hybridization hypothesis [18].
117.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TcV and TcVI cannot be differentiated using the PCR-RFLP assay based on the TcSC5D gene. The option incorrectly claims that the assay can resolve all lineages with a single step. The passage also describes a second PCR-RFLP assay using a different locus (TcMK) to distinguish TcV and TcVI, which is not included in the single-step claim.

117.Option: The proposed PCR-RFLP assay can discriminate all non-hybrid lineages of T. cruzi and can also detect mixed parasite stocks, with the exception of a 50:50 mixture of TcII and TcIII strains, which would be indistinguishable from a TcV/TcVI hybrid.
Evidence: These patterns are unique for all DTUs, except for DTUs TcV and TcVI, that cannot be differentiated.
In the case of these DTUs, the banding pattern is essentially the sum of the patterns of DTUs TcII and TcIII, which agrees with the currently favored hybridization hypothesis [18].
117.Rationale: The passage states that the PCR-RFLP assay can discriminate all non-hybrid lineages (TcI, TcII, TcIII, TcIV, Tcbat) with unique patterns. It also explains that hybrid lineages TcV and TcVI produce a banding pattern that is the sum of their parental lineages TcII and TcIII. This means a 50:50 mixture of TcII and TcIII would produce the same pattern as a TcV/TcVI hybrid. This matches the option's claim about the assay's capabilities and limitations.

117.Option: The PCR-RFLP assay can reliably distinguish between TcV and TcVI lineages by detecting a unique restriction fragment pattern that reflects their distinct evolutionary origins from TcII and TcIII.
Evidence: These patterns are unique for all DTUs, except for DTUs TcV and TcVI, that cannot be differentiated.
In the case of these DTUs, the banding pattern is essentially the sum of the patterns of DTUs TcII and TcIII, which agrees with the currently favored hybridization hypothesis [18].
117.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that TcV and TcVI cannot be distinguished using the PCR-RFLP assay based on the TcSC5D gene. The option incorrectly claims that the assay can distinguish them by detecting a unique pattern. The passage also describes a second PCR-RFLP assay using a different locus (TcMK) to distinguish TcV and TcVI, which is not included in this option.

118.Option: Peptide OR24 P2 showed promising results with sera of dogs and goats, and may replace salivary gland homogenate or recombinant proteins in surveillance for anti-Phlebotomus orientalis IgG.
Evidence: Peptide OR24 P2 had a high correlation coefficient with the SGH for dog and goat sera. It reached comparable PPV, NPV, specificity, and sensitivity as OR24 P1 for dogs and the highest PPV, NPV, specificity and sensitivity for goats (all above 0.75).
The correlation analysis for the most promising peptide (OR24 P2) with dogs and goats is shown in Fig 3.
118.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that OR24 P2 demonstrated high correlation coefficients, PPV, NPV, specificity, and sensitivity in dogs and goats. These statistical values indicate that the peptide performs well as an antigen for detecting anti-P. orientalis IgG. The passage also mentions that the goal of the study was to compare peptides with recombinant proteins and assess their applicability for large-scale surveillance. The strong performance of OR24 P2 in these host species supports the inference that it could potentially replace SGH or recombinant proteins in surveillance efforts.

118.Option: The high species specificity of the peptides means they can be used to distinguish between different sand fly species in mixed-infestation areas without prior knowledge of exposure history.
Evidence: Significant differences in OD values were detected with sera of mice bitten by P. orientalis compared to all the other three groups, as shown in Fig 2. No differences were observed in non-exposed controls and mice exposed to P. papatasi or S. schwetzi (Fig 2).

This suggests that the peptides can distinguish between different sand fly species in mixed-infestation areas. The ability to detect species-specific responses without prior knowledge of exposure history is a key advantage for field surveillance.

118.Option: Peptide OR24 P2 is the most effective antigen for detecting anti-sand fly IgG in humans, based on its high sensitivity and specificity in domestic animals.
Evidence: Peptide OR24 P2 had a high correlation coefficient with the SGH for dog and goat sera. It reached comparable PPV, NPV, specificity, and sensitivity as OR24 P1 for dogs and the highest PPV, NPV, specificity and sensitivity for goats (all above 0.75).
118.Rationale: While the passage provides evidence that OR24 P2 performed well in domestic animals (dogs and goats), it does not provide any data on its performance in humans. The inference that it is the most effective antigen for detecting anti-sand fly IgG in humans is not supported by the evidence in the passage. The passage only discusses the use of the peptides in domestic animals and does not extrapolate these results to humans.

118.Option: The replacement of salivary gland homogenate with synthetic peptides is feasible only in dogs and goats because of the high cost and complexity of peptide production for other host species.
Evidence: Peptide design and preparation: Peptides were designed from amino acid sequences based on the two most suitable recombinant proteins of P. orientalis (rPorSP24 and rPorSP65) as previously described [21]. Two peptides from each protein were selected in the software EpiQuest-B.

These four generated sequences (Table 1) were sent to a commercial laboratory (Genosphere Biotechnologies, France), where they were synthesised and conjugated with two molecules of polyethylene glycol, which acts as a spacer on ELISA plates and facilitates improved accessibility of antibodies.
118.Rationale: The passage does not mention any high cost or complexity of peptide production for other host species. Instead, it describes a standardized process for peptide design and synthesis that could be applied to various host species. The study tested the peptides in dogs, goats, and sheep, indicating that the methodology is not limited to just dogs and goats. The feasibility of replacing SGH with synthetic peptides is not restricted to specific host species based on the information provided.

118.Option: The use of synthetic peptides for detecting anti-sand fly IgG responses is superior to recombinant proteins in all host species tested, including sheep, due to higher specificity and lower cross-reactivity.
Evidence: With sheep sera, all statistical values were very high (> 0.9) except for the low sensitivity (0.25).

Despite high statistical values for OR65 P2 and canine sera (0.8), high cut-off and the lowest correlation coefficient (0.65) were observed with this host species. In contrast, OR65 P2 was the second best antigen for goats, with the lowest cut-off value, high correlation, PPV, NPV, specificity, and sensitivity (all above 0.65). Although high PPV, NPV, and specificity (> 0.9) were detected with sheep sera, this peptide achieved low correlation and NPV (< 0.6).
118.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the performance of synthetic peptides varied across different host species. For sheep, some peptides showed high specificity and PPV but low sensitivity and correlation. This indicates that synthetic peptides are not universally superior to recombinant proteins in all host species tested. The claim that they are superior in all host species, including sheep, is not supported by the evidence in the passage.

119.Option: Malathion selection had a negative effect on resistance to other insecticides, including temephos and deltamethrin, suggesting that resistance mechanisms to malathion may divert metabolic resources away from resistance to other classes of insecticides.
Evidence: Selection with malathion did not induce cross-resistance with temephos, although both are OP (Tables 1 and S3).
In particular, temephos resistance reduction was more pronounced in Crato, the population with the highest initial RR.
In opposition, malathion selected groups, and particularly Crato mosquitoes, exhibited reduction of deltamethrin resistance levels.
119.Rationale: The passage states that malathion selection did not induce cross-resistance with temephos and actually reduced temephos resistance in some cases. It also notes a reduction in deltamethrin resistance in malathion-selected groups. These findings contradict the claim that malathion resistance mechanisms divert resources away from other resistance mechanisms. Instead, the evidence suggests that malathion selection can reduce resistance to other insecticides, not that it competes for resources.

119.Option: The lack of changes in acetylcholinesterase (Ace) activity suggests that malathion resistance in A. aegypti is primarily mediated by metabolic detoxification rather than target-site insensitivity.
Evidence: Total activity of the OP target site, AChE, remained equivalent to the original samples.

We also investigated Ace using the WHO susceptibility criterion: inhibition of 70% or more of Ace's activity with propoxur (AChI); in all cases the results are indicative of a susceptible enzyme (Tables 3, 4 and S7). According to this criterion, Ace of both parental Aracaju and Crato populations has a susceptible profile.
119.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that there were no changes in acetylcholinesterase (Ace) activity in the malathion-selected populations compared to the original samples. This directly supports the inference that malathion resistance is not due to target-site insensitivity (which would involve changes in Ace activity) but is instead likely mediated by other mechanisms such as metabolic detoxification. This conclusion is consistent with the evidence provided in the biochemical assays section.

119.Option: The higher metabolic resistance observed in adult mosquitoes compared to larvae suggests that adult-stage selection would be more effective in generating cross-resistance to other insecticides.
Evidence: For both populations more changes and higher activities were noted in adult mosquitoes, compared to the larval stage, in all C and S groups. The exceptions were α-EST and β-EST activities, which are higher in larvae (S7 Table).
In general, no differences were observed between control groups and those exposed to malathion which could be classified as diagnostic of the selected resistance.
119.Rationale: While the passage notes higher metabolic resistance in adult mosquitoes compared to larvae, it does not provide evidence that adult-stage selection is more effective in generating cross-resistance to other insecticides. In fact, the study found that malathion selection in larvae reduced resistance to deltamethrin, not increased it. The option makes an unsupported inference about the effectiveness of adult-stage selection for cross-resistance, which is not directly addressed in the passage.

119.Option: The reduction in deltamethrin resistance in malathion-selected populations indicates that malathion and deltamethrin share a common resistance mechanism, leading to competitive inhibition.
Evidence: In opposition, malathion selected groups, and particularly Crato mosquitoes, exhibited reduction of deltamethrin resistance levels.

The simple laboratory rearing, in the absence of any insecticide (groups C1, C2), exacerbated PY resistance levels in both populations (Tables 1 and S5). In particular, the SR index confirmed the marked deltamethrin resistance increase in the control groups, mainly in Aracaju (S5 Table). This increase in deltamethrin resistance, however, was not observed in the S1-3 groups, selected with malathion, independently of the analytical method adopted (RR, SR and 95% confidence limits overlapping).
119.Rationale: The passage states that malathion selection reduced deltamethrin resistance, while control groups (without insecticide) showed increased deltamethrin resistance. This suggests that malathion selection may not share a common resistance mechanism with deltamethrin, but rather that the absence of selection pressure in control groups allowed resistance to increase. The option's claim about competitive inhibition and shared mechanisms is not supported by the evidence, which instead suggests that malathion selection may have a different effect on resistance mechanisms than the control groups.

120.Option: The high correlation between pathogenic Leptospira species and large agricultural animals such as cattle and water buffalo in Kandy suggests that these animals are the primary source of environmental contamination in paddy fields, despite the potential for indirect associations due to resuspension of soil particles.
Evidence: Among these vertebrates, we found that 13 and 1 species showed significant correlation with the eDNA detection of Leptospira in K and GK samples, respectively (Fig 4). These included animals such as dog (Canis lupus familiaris), sheep (Ovis aries), Indian civet (Viverricula indica), cattle (Bos indicus), chicken (Gallus gallus), porcupine (Hystrix sp.), water buffalo (Bubalus bubalis) (r > 0.632, d.f. = 8, p < 0.05; Benjamini–Hochberg [BH]-corrected false discovery rate [FDR] q < 0.01, r > 0.786 and r > 0.982 in K and GK, respectively) exhibiting significant correlation with at least one Operational Taxonomic Unit (OTU) of Leptospira.

cattle exhibited high correlation with several species of Leptospira (e.g., r = 0.896, d.f. = 8, p = 0.003 with L. interrogans group and r = 0.922, d.f. = 8, p = 0.001 with L. noguchii CZ214), whereas chicken and water buffalo showed high correlation with a particular OTU of Leptospira in Kandy (r = 0.919, d.f. = 8, p = 0.001 with L. neocaledonica group and r = 0.858, d.f. = 8, p = 0.006 with L. borgpetersenii group, respectively).

In Sri Lanka, cattle demonstrate a high seropositive rate for leptospirosis [19, 20]. Furthermore, pathogenic Leptospira have been detected either by isolation or molecular techniques in a wide range of reservoir animals such as bandicoots, rats, dogs, and domesticated elephants [21, 22]. A meta-analysis to estimate the load of Leptospira excreted in urine had also shown that large mammals which excrete much more urine than rats shed significantly more Leptospira per day [23]. Thus, in agrarian communities, large animals such as buffalo and cattle might be the main source excreting pathogenic leptospires into paddy lands.
120.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this inference. First, the study found significant correlations between pathogenic Leptospira and large agricultural animals like cattle and water buffalo in Kandy (r values up to 0.922). Second, the passage explicitly states that cattle have a high seropositive rate for leptospirosis in Sri Lanka. Third, a meta-analysis cited in the passage shows that large mammals excrete significantly more Leptospira per day than rats. Finally, the passage directly concludes that in agrarian communities, large animals like buffalo and cattle are the main sources of pathogenic Leptospira in paddy lands. The option acknowledges the potential for indirect associations (like resuspension of soil particles) while still affirming the primary role of these animals, which is consistent with the evidence.

120.Option: The detection of human eDNA in irrigation water samples from Kandy and Girandurukotte indicates that human exposure to pathogenic Leptospira is primarily driven by direct contact with contaminated water during bathing and laundry activities.
Evidence: Among these vertebrates, we found that 13 and 1 species showed significant correlation with the eDNA detection of Leptospira in K and GK samples, respectively (Fig 4). These included animals such as dog (Canis lupus familiaris), sheep (Ovis aries), Indian civet (Viverricula indica), cattle (Bos indicus), chicken (Gallus gallus), porcupine (Hystrix sp.), water buffalo (Bubalus bubalis) (r > 0.632, d.f. = 8, p < 0.05; Benjamini–Hochberg [BH]-corrected false discovery rate [FDR] q < 0.01, r > 0.786 and r > 0.982 in K and GK, respectively) exhibiting significant correlation with at least one Operational Taxonomic Unit (OTU) of Leptospira.
Boar (Sus scrofa), human (Homo sapiens), and rats (Rattus sp.) indicated positive but not significant correlations (r > 0.3) with some Leptospira.
120.Rationale: While the passage does mention the detection of human eDNA in irrigation water samples, it only indicates a positive but not significant correlation (r > 0.3) with some Leptospira species. The option makes a strong claim that human exposure is 'primarily driven by' bathing and laundry activities, which is not supported by the data presented. The passage focuses on agricultural activities as the main risk factor for leptospirosis in Sri Lanka, noting that 80% of suspected cases had exposure to paddy and marshy lands and 60% were farmers. The evidence does not support the specific claim about bathing and laundry activities being the primary driver of human exposure.

120.Option: The absence of significant correlation between pathogenic Leptospira and tetrapod animals in Girandurukotte suggests that the P1 subclade of Leptospira is not capable of sustained environmental transmission without mammalian reservoirs.
Evidence: In GK samples, on the other hand, there were no tetrapod animals that appeared repeatedly and exhibited high correlation with Leptospira detection.

The etiological agents of leptospirosis in Sri Lanka belong to species of the P1 subclade/virulent group (L. interrogans, L. kirschneri, L. borgpetersenii, L. santorasai and L. weilii) [22, 28]. Little to no information is available regarding the environmental Leptospira species of Sri Lanka.
120.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence to support this conclusion. The absence of significant correlations in Girandurukotte could be due to various factors such as lower sampling intensity, different environmental conditions, or methodological limitations. The passage does not state that P1 subclade Leptospira cannot sustain environmental transmission without mammalian reservoirs. In fact, it mentions that pathogenic Leptospira can survive in the environment for weeks to months depending on climatic factors and soil conditions. The option makes an overgeneralization that cannot be derived from the evidence presented.

120.Option: The high correlation between Arcobacter cryaerophilus and pathogenic Leptospira in Kandy indicates that this bacterium is a key facilitator of Leptospira transmission in irrigation water through biofilm formation.
Evidence: Arcobacter cryaerophilus (OTU number 3316 in GreenGenes database) shows the highest correlation in K samples (r = 0.971, d.f. = 8, p < 0.001) and Acinetobacter johnsonii (OTU number 3871) show higher correlation (r = 0.767, d.f. = 8, p = 0.010) with P1 subclade than P2 subclade.

Bacteria excreted into the environment are able to form biofilms by interacting with other organisms which might be a strategy to maintain a sufficiently concentrated amount of bacteria in order to achieve infection [26, 27]. The composition of microbial communities, which is dependent on the climate and soil properties of each agro-ecological region, also influences the formation of biofilms, and thus, the persistence of infectious Leptospira in the environment.
120.Rationale: While the passage does show a high correlation between Arcobacter cryaerophilus and pathogenic Leptospira in Kandy, it does not provide direct evidence that this bacterium is a 'key facilitator' of Leptospira transmission through biofilm formation. The passage mentions that bacteria can form biofilms which might help maintain bacterial concentrations, but it does not specifically link Arcobacter cryaerophilus to this process. The option makes a causal claim that cannot be directly inferred from the correlation data presented. The passage also notes that partial correlations corrected by A. cryaerophilus showed reduced values between vertebrates and Leptospira, suggesting a complex relationship that cannot be simplified to a key facilitator role.

121.Option: The use of vaccination cards as a verification method likely underestimates true coverage because many residents may have lost or not received them, leading to an overestimation of recall-based coverage.
Evidence: OCV coverage by respondents’ recall was 91% (95% CI: 88%‒93%) for the first dose and 85% (95% CI: 81%‒89%) for the second dose, among the 1,102 individuals aged ≥1 year in households surveyed at first follow-up. Vaccination cards were available for 443 (40%) individuals. OCV coverage by card, among individuals with available documentation, was 93% (95% CI: 89%‒96%) for the first dose and 84% (95% CI: 78%‒89%) for the second dose (Table 6).
Absence from the camp was the most commonly reported reason both for non-vaccination (14%) and receipt of only 1 OCV dose (6%), besides non-eligibility.
121.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence of a discrepancy between recall-based coverage (91% and 85%) and card-based coverage (93% and 84%) for the first and second doses, respectively. The fact that vaccination cards were only available for 40% of individuals indicates that the card-based verification method cannot account for the full population. The higher recall-based coverage suggests that many residents may have received the vaccine but lost or did not have access to their cards. This supports the inference that vaccination cards likely underestimate true coverage, while recall-based reporting may overestimate it due to potential inaccuracies in memory or social desirability bias.

121.Option: The high anticipated acceptability and OCV campaign coverage observed were similar to evaluations from other OCV campaigns, but higher than previously published coverage estimates from the Maela OCV campaign based on a vaccination registry.
Evidence: OCV coverage by respondents’ recall was 91% (95% CI: 88%‒93%) for the first dose and 85% (95% CI: 81%‒89%) for the second dose, among the 1,102 individuals aged ≥1 year in households surveyed at first follow-up. Vaccination cards were available for 443 (40%) individuals. OCV coverage by card, among individuals with available documentation, was 93% (95% CI: 89%‒96%) for the first dose and 84% (95% CI: 78%‒89%) for the second dose (Table 6).
At baseline, 262 (97%) of 271 respondents reported willingness to receive OCV, and 221 (97%) of 228 of respondents with children reported willingness to let their child receive OCV.
121.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about coverage estimates from a vaccination registry or comparisons with other OCV campaigns. While it does mention high anticipated acceptability (97% willingness to receive OCV at baseline), it does not compare these results to other campaigns or to a vaccination registry. The option introduces external comparisons not supported by the passage, making it an incorrect inference.

121.Option: The high OCV coverage reported in the survey may be inflated due to social desirability bias, as interviewers were community health workers from the same camp population.
Evidence: The survey was conducted by Maela refugees who were trained and working as PU-AMI community health workers (CHWs).

OCV coverage by respondents’ recall was 91% (95% CI: 88%‒93%) for the first dose and 85% (95% CI: 81%‒89%) for the second dose, among the 1,102 individuals aged ≥1 year in households surveyed at first follow-up.
121.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the survey was conducted by community health workers from the same camp population. This creates a potential for social desirability bias, where respondents may overreport vaccine uptake to appear cooperative or aligned with the health workers. The high recall-based coverage (91% and 85%) reported in the survey supports the inference that the coverage estimates may be inflated due to this bias.

121.Option: The discrepancy between survey-based coverage and registry-based coverage suggests that the vaccination registry was poorly maintained or inaccurate, undermining its reliability as a data source.
Evidence: OCV coverage by respondents’ recall was 91% (95% CI: 88%‒93%) for the first dose and 85% (95% CI: 81%‒89%) for the second dose, among the 1,102 individuals aged ≥1 year in households surveyed at first follow-up. Vaccination cards were available for 443 (40%) individuals. OCV coverage by card, among individuals with available documentation, was 93% (95% CI: 89%‒96%) for the first dose and 84% (95% CI: 78%‒89%) for the second dose (Table 6).
121.Rationale: The passage does not provide any information about a vaccination registry. It only mentions vaccination cards as a verification method. The option introduces the concept of a registry-based coverage estimate, which is not mentioned in the passage. Therefore, the inference about the vaccination registry being poorly maintained or inaccurate cannot be derived from the passage.

122.Option: The cytosolic phospholipase A2 (PLA2c) is the sole enzyme responsible for prostaglandin synthesis in mosquitoes, as its knockdown completely abolishes PG production and immune function.
Evidence: The silencing of PLA2s significantly decreased in 70% the production of PG in the midgut, whereas the silencing of PLA2c did not affect PGs production (Fig 5C).
122.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that silencing PLA2c did not affect prostaglandin production, while silencing PLA2s reduced it by 70%. This directly contradicts the claim that PLA2c is the sole enzyme responsible for PG synthesis. The evidence shows that multiple enzymes contribute to PG synthesis in mosquitoes, with PLA2s playing a more significant role than PLA2c.

122.Option: The inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis leads to a complete failure of the mosquito immune system, rendering it incapable of mounting any defense against pathogens.
Evidence: In ASA-pretreated Aag2 cells, 99 immune-related genes were suppressed (S2 Dataset).

The expression of serpin 27A and gambicin were independent of prostaglandin production, both in cells and mosquitoes, and treatment with ASA did not alter their expression in comparison to blood-fed non-ASA treated females as observed in the microarray (Fig 3D and 3E).
122.Rationale: The passage shows that while prostaglandin inhibition suppresses many immune genes, some immune responses remain functional. Specifically, serpin 27A and gambicin expression were unaffected by ASA treatment. This indicates that the mosquito immune system is not completely disabled by PG inhibition, but rather partially compromised. The claim of 'complete failure' is therefore not supported by the evidence.

122.Option: Prostaglandins modulate the amplitude of the immune response in mosquitoes, rather than being responsible for its activation.
Evidence: In the most severe cases, a person may require: - Additional oxygen, - IV fluids if they can't drink enough to stay hydrated, or - Intubation (have a breathing tube inserted through the mouth and down to the airway) with mechanical ventilation (a machine to help a person breathe).
In most of these cases, hospitalization lasts only a few days.
122.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that prostaglandins are involved in the initial activation of immune pathways. Instead, it shows that PG inhibition reduces the expression of immune genes like defensin A and cecropin G, suggesting that PGs modulate the amplitude of the immune response rather than initiating it. The evidence supports the idea that PGs act as modulators of immune response strength rather than activators.

122.Option: Prostaglandins function as primary signaling molecules that directly trigger the Toll and IMD pathways in response to microbial infection in mosquitoes.
Evidence: The recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) turns the switch on, and its clearance turns it off.
Activation of Toll and IMD leads to the production of AMPs and other effector molecules that participate in the pathogen killing.
122.Rationale: The passage describes an on/off switch model where PAMP recognition activates immune pathways. It shows that PG inhibition reduces the expression of immune genes regulated by Toll and IMD pathways, but does not indicate that PGs directly trigger these pathways. The evidence suggests PGs modulate rather than initiate pathway activation, making this option inconsistent with the passage.

122.Option: Prostaglandins are essential for the initial activation of immune pathways in mosquitoes, as their absence prevents the induction of immune genes in response to bacterial challenge.
Evidence: The recognition of pathogen-associated molecular patterns (PAMPs) turns the switch on, and its clearance turns it off.
In ASA-pretreated Aag2 cells, Ec-challenge induced the upregulation of 361 genes, whereas 822 were down-regulated (Fig 2A, S2 Fig and S1 Dataset).
122.Rationale: The passage shows that immune genes are still upregulated in ASA-treated cells (361 genes) despite PG inhibition, indicating that immune pathways can still be activated. The evidence contradicts the claim that PGs are essential for initial activation, as immune gene induction occurs even in their absence. The on/off switch model also suggests PAMP recognition, not PGs, is the primary activation mechanism.

123.Option: The use of a stable emulsion containing GLA, a synthetic glucopyranosyl lipid A molecule similar to the naturally derived MPL®, ensures consistent and universal protection across all Leishmania species and transmission routes.
Evidence: More than one lot of each antigen was used in experiments with a residual endotoxin content ranging from 77 to 245 EU/mg protein for L110f and from <0.05 to 27 EU/mg protein for KSAC.
KSAC+GLA-SE, but not L110f+GLA-SLE immunized mice displayed significant protection up to the final 8 week time point (Figure 2C).
123.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that GLA-SE ensures 'consistent and universal protection across all Leishmania species and transmission routes.' The data show that while KSAC+GLA-SE provided significant protection, L110f+GLA-SE did not confer the same level of protection. Additionally, the study only focused on L. major and did not address other Leishmania species. The claim of 'universal protection' is an overgeneralization not supported by the evidence presented.

123.Option: The robust antibody response and positive IgG2a∶IgG1 ratio induced by KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination are sufficient to confer protection against vector-transmitted L. major infection, independent of T cell responses.
Evidence: KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination induced the production of KSAC-specific IFN-γ+ CD4+ T cells, and the relative size of this cell fraction was maintained after a challenge with L. major-infected sand flies (Fig. 3A).
Mice vaccinated with KSAC+GLA-SE produced a high level of antigen-specific IFN-γ following ex vivo stimulation with SLA or KSAC both pre- and post-challenge with infected flies (Figure 4A).
123.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that T cell responses, particularly IFN-γ production by CD4+ T cells, are a significant component of the protective immune response. The option incorrectly claims that antibody responses alone are sufficient for protection. The data show that both antibody responses and T cell responses are involved in the protective effect of KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination.

123.Option: The partial protection observed in L110f-vaccinated mice following either needle or fly challenge with the Friedlin-V1 strain was abrogated when the mice were challenged with sand flies carrying the virulent WR-2885 strain, indicating that the L110f+GLA-SE vaccine is ineffective against field isolates of L. major.
Evidence: To assess the relevance of the parasite strain used in challenge, we carried out a preliminary experiment using a recent field isolate of L. major (WR-2885)... KSAC-immunized mice were protected while L110f-immunized mice lost the partial protection displayed against the Friedlin V1 laboratory attenuated parasite strain (Figure S1).
123.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that L110f-immunized mice lost the partial protection they had against the Friedlin V1 strain when challenged with the WR-2885 field isolate. This directly supports the inference that the L110f+GLA-SE vaccine is ineffective against field isolates of L. major.

123.Option: The lack of pre-challenge IFN-γ production in L110f+GLA-SE-immunized mice suggests that the vaccine induces a delayed but ultimately effective immune response that is sufficient for protection against vector-transmitted infection.
Evidence: L110f+GLA-SE-immunized mice produced IFN-γ only after the mice were challenged with infected flies (Figure 3B).
L110f+GLA-SE immunized mice showed a significant reduction of parasite number (P<0.01) compared to GLA-SE immunized mice (Figure 2D).
123.Rationale: The passage shows that L110f+GLA-SE immunized mice did produce IFN-γ after challenge, but the level of protection was lower than that of KSAC+GLA-SE. However, the option incorrectly suggests that the delayed IFN-γ production is 'sufficient for protection.' The data show that while there was some protection, it was not as effective as the KSAC vaccine, and the option overstates the effectiveness of the L110f vaccine.

123.Option: Immunization with KSAC in combination with the TLR4 agonist GLA in stable emulsion overcomes the Th2 bias of BALB/c mice, generating a robust, cell-mediated Th1 immune response that results in solid protection against vector-transmitted L. major infection.
Evidence: It has been long established that protection from Leishmania parasites requires the induction of a Th1 immune response [16], [17], [18].
BALB/c mice produce a polarized Th2 type immune response against Leishmania spp. and are used extensively to test Leishmania antigens [19].

KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination induced the production of KSAC-specific IFN-γ+ CD4+ T cells, and the relative size of this cell fraction was maintained after a challenge with L. major-infected sand flies (Fig. 3A).
Mice vaccinated with KSAC+GLA-SE produced a high level of antigen-specific IFN-γ following ex vivo stimulation with SLA or KSAC both pre- and post-challenge with infected flies (Figure 4A).
123.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that KSAC+GLA-SE vaccination overcomes the Th2 bias of BALB/c mice by inducing a Th1 immune response, as evidenced by the production of IFN-γ by CD4+ T cells. This Th1 response is associated with solid protection against vector-transmitted L. major infection, as demonstrated by the significant reduction in parasite burden and lesion size in vaccinated mice.

124.Option: The emergence of the China I lineage in the third epidemic wave indicates that the virus has undergone a significant evolutionary shift, rendering previous control measures obsolete.
Evidence: The phylogenetic analysis predicted six major clades (China I - China VI) with high statistical support and which appeared to show differences in geographical composition.

China I can be further divided into subclades I-A and I-B with high support (Figure 4 and Table S1). The I-A subclade contains almost all of the China I strains, and can be further divided into eight branches, also with strong support and are of primary interest.

The MCC tree of 320 complete G sequences of rabies street strains in China. Consistent with earlier studies, isolates are classified into six major clades China I to VI, with China I and II containing the majority of samples.
124.Rationale: The passage describes the emergence of the China I lineage as a dominant clade in the third epidemic wave but does not provide evidence that this represents a significant evolutionary shift that rendered previous control measures obsolete. The text states that China I is now the dominant clade and has expanded geographically, but it does not claim that this lineage is fundamentally different from previous ones in terms of pathogenicity or resistance to control measures. The evidence shows a shift in geographic distribution and lineage prevalence, not an evolutionary shift that would necessitate abandoning previous control strategies.

124.Option: The recent decline in human rabies cases in China is due to improved surveillance and reporting, rather than effective public health interventions.
Evidence: The number of human cases continued to increase in medium and low incidence regions after the number of national cases decreased

The program for collection of human brain samples was approved by the Ethical Committee of the National Institute of Viral Disease Control and Prevention, China CDC, which is the national referral center for rabies diagnosis.
However, in the cases when laboratory diagnosis is performed, it was found there was a very strong correlation between positive outcome and clinical diagnosis
124.Rationale: The passage does not support the claim that the decline in cases is due to improved surveillance rather than public health interventions. In fact, it states that the decline in national cases occurred while cases in medium and low incidence regions were still increasing, suggesting a real geographic shift rather than an artifact of surveillance. The text also notes that surveillance methods have remained consistent over time, and the decline in cases is described as a genuine trend rather than an artifact of reporting practices.

124.Option: The implementation of a nationwide dog culling program in 1984 led to a rapid decline in human rabies cases and effectively controlled the second epidemic.
Evidence: Red arrow indicates the beginning of a concerted dog culling program implemented in 1984.

The second wave extended from the 1960s to the middle of 1990s and was the most serious epidemic with 4000–7000 cases reported annually for most of the 1980s, with a peak of 7037 cases occurring in 1981.

China is currently in the midst of a third wave that peaked in 2007 (3300 cases). Since then, the numbers have begun to gradually decrease, although there are still around 2000 cases reported every year
124.Rationale: The passage mentions the implementation of a dog culling program in 1984 but does not state that it led to a rapid decline in cases or effectively controlled the second epidemic. In fact, the second wave continued until the mid-1990s, with high case numbers persisting through the 1980s. The text does not provide evidence that the culling program was effective in controlling the epidemic, only that it was implemented during the second wave.

124.Option: The rapid emergence of a third epidemic wave so rapidly after the second epidemic had been controlled highlights the challenge of completely eradicating rabies.
Evidence: The gap between successive epidemics is brief, with a rapid reemergence of cases within a few years.
The country is currently in the midst of a third epidemic that begun in 1996 (159 cases), peaked in 2007 (3300 cases) and has begun to gradually decrease since this time.
Thus, rabies remains a serious public health problem in China with the continuing threat of a resurgence of the disease.
124.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the gap between successive epidemics is brief and that there is a rapid reemergence of cases within a few years. The description of the third epidemic emerging shortly after the second epidemic had seemingly been controlled directly supports the inference that rabies is difficult to eradicate and has a tendency to resurge. This pattern of recurring epidemics, despite control efforts, highlights the persistent challenge of eliminating the disease.

124.Option: The geographic spread of rabies from high to low incidence regions in China is primarily driven by the movement of wildlife, particularly ferret badgers and deer.
Evidence: We concluded that the spread of rabies viruses from high incidence regions was facilitated by the long-distance movement or trans-provincial translocation of dogs associated with human-related activities and this was likely one of the factors contributing to the rapid increase in human rabies cases in new regions

The majority of isolates are from dogs, but human isolates are marked with an X (left column) domesticated animals such as cattle are marked with a blue square (middle column) and wildlife are marked with a circle (right column).
Wildlife isolates are almost exclusively isolated with older lineages
124.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the spread of rabies in China is primarily driven by the movement of dogs associated with human-related activities, not by wildlife. While the text mentions ferret badgers and deer as hosts, it emphasizes that the majority of isolates are from dogs. The evidence directly contradicts the claim that wildlife is the primary driver of geographic spread, making this option incorrect.

125.Option: CxFV Izabal and WNV exhibit competitive exclusion in Cx. quinquefasciatus due to shared replication sites and limited cellular resources.
Evidence: Growth of WNV was not inhibited by CxFV Izabal in either C6/36 cells or Cx. quinquefasciatus Sebring mosquitoes (Figs 4,5).

WNV titers were not significantly different between CxFV Izabal (+), CxFV Izabal (−) or mock-inoculated Cx. quinquefasciatus at 1, 2, 8, or 10 days following per os infection with WNV (p>0.05) (Fig. 5).

The percentage of CxFV (+) and CxFV (−) Cx. quinquefasciatus that became infected, developed a disseminated infection, and transmitted WNV were not significantly different for either mosquito strain or any WNV blood meal titer examined (Fisher Exact test, p>0.05, Table 2).

WNV infection, dissemination, and transmission rates in mosquitoes inoculated with heat-inactivated CxFV Izabal did not differ significantly from those inoculated with live CxFV Izabal or uninfected with CxFV Izabal (Table 2).
125.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that CxFV Izabal does not inhibit WNV growth in either cell culture or mosquitoes. The data show no significant differences in WNV titers or transmission rates between CxFV-infected and uninfected mosquitoes. The concept of competitive exclusion would predict reduced replication of one virus in the presence of the other, but the experimental results do not support this. The evidence directly contradicts the claim of competitive exclusion.

125.Option: CxFV Izabal does not interfere with the growth or transmission of WNV in Cx. quinquefasciatus, despite the potential for co-infection in the same tissues.
Evidence: Growth of WNV was not inhibited by CxFV Izabal in either C6/36 cells or Cx. quinquefasciatus Sebring mosquitoes (Figs 4,5).

WNV titers were not significantly different between CxFV Izabal (+), CxFV Izabal (−) or mock-inoculated Cx. quinquefasciatus at 1, 2, 8, or 10 days following per os infection with WNV (p>0.05) (Fig. 5).

The percentage of CxFV (+) and CxFV (−) Cx. quinquefasciatus that became infected, developed a disseminated infection, and transmitted WNV were not significantly different for either mosquito strain or any WNV blood meal titer examined (Fisher Exact test, p>0.05, Table 2).

WNV infection, dissemination, and transmission rates in mosquitoes inoculated with heat-inactivated CxFV Izabal did not differ significantly from those inoculated with live CxFV Izabal or uninfected with CxFV Izabal (Table 2).
125.Rationale: The passage provides multiple lines of evidence supporting this conclusion. The study found no significant differences in WNV growth, infection rates, dissemination, or transmission between CxFV-infected and uninfected mosquitoes. The data show that CxFV does not interfere with WNV despite potential for co-infection in the same tissues. This conclusion is directly supported by the experimental results presented in the passage.

125.Option: Prior infection with CxFV Izabal induces a long-lasting RNA interference response in Cx. quinquefasciatus that suppresses WNV replication.
Evidence: WNV titers were not significantly different between CxFV Izabal (+), CxFV Izabal (−) or mock-inoculated Cx. quinquefasciatus at 1, 2, 8, or 10 days following per os infection with WNV (p>0.05) (Fig. 5).

The percentage of CxFV (+) and CxFV (−) Cx. quinquefasciatus that became infected, developed a disseminated infection, and transmitted WNV were not significantly different for either mosquito strain or any WNV blood meal titer examined (Fisher Exact test, p>0.05, Table 2).

WNV infection, dissemination, and transmission rates in mosquitoes inoculated with heat-inactivated CxFV Izabal did not differ significantly from those inoculated with live CxFV Izabal or uninfected with CxFV Izabal (Table 2).
125.Rationale: The passage provides no evidence of RNA interference or any long-lasting antiviral response induced by CxFV Izabal. In fact, the data show that WNV replication and transmission are not suppressed in CxFV-infected mosquitoes. The claim about RNA interference is not supported by the experimental results presented in the passage.

125.Option: CxFV Izabal may enhance WNV transmission in some mosquito populations by piggybacking on WNV to infect salivary glands.
Evidence: A significantly higher percentage of Honduras Cx. quinquefasciatus transmitted WNV when co-inoculated simultaneously with CxFV Izabal (98%, n = 53) than when inoculated with WNV alone (69%, n = 36) (p = 0.0014, Fisher Exact test) (Fig. 6).
Midgut (Fig. 7) and head tissues (Fig. 8) of mosquitoes inoculated simultaneously with CxFV Izabal and WNV were observed to be infected with both viruses by IFA.

Co-infected head tissues of Cx. quinquefasciatus. Mosquito inoculated simultaneously with CxFV Izabal and WNV, harvested 9 DPI. CxFV Izabal stained with AlexaFluor 594 (red) and WNV with Alexa 488 (green).
125.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that CxFV Izabal may enhance WNV transmission in some mosquito populations. Specifically, the study found a significantly higher percentage of Honduras Cx. quinquefasciatus transmitting WNV when co-inoculated with CxFV Izabal compared to those inoculated with WNV alone. The data also show that mosquitoes inoculated simultaneously with both viruses were infected with both viruses in their midgut and head tissues. This suggests a potential mechanism where CxFV may 'piggyback' on WNV to infect salivary glands, thereby enhancing transmission in certain populations.

126.Option: The primary mechanism of vascular disruption in leptospirosis is immune-mediated, with host inflammatory responses causing endothelial damage after Leptospira infection is established.
