333.Option: In endemic areas of intestinal schistosomiasis with low-intensity infections, the actual prevalence can be underestimated by up to 4.7 times when measured by the recommended standard procedure.
Evidence: The reference standard was used to evaluate the efficacy of each of the diagnostic methods tested. For the fecal techniques, the best performance was obtained with the modified Helmintex method, which identified schistosome eggs in feces of 88 individuals (40.4% of prevalence). This parasitological method showed a high sensitivity (86.6%) and the highest degree of concordance in relation to the reference standard (kappa = 0.84) (Table 3). The analysis also demonstrated that the sensitivity of the KK method increased from 41.4% with two slides from one fecal sample to up to 66.7% with six slides from three fecal samples. In comparison, if only one fecal sample was processed, the sensitivity remained around 60%, even when the number of examined slides was increased to 12 or 14 (Table 3 and Fig 3). The improved performance of the KK method due to an increased number of examined slides (14 slides) or increased sampling effort (three fecal samples) is shown in Fig 3.

Prevalence of Schistosoma mansoni infection (white bars), as determined by the analysis of one fecal sample with two (SPL1 K1-K2), six (SPL1 K1-K6), 12 (SPL1 K1-K12) or 14 slides (SPL1 K1-K14), or obtained with the analysis of two slides prepared from each of three fecal samples (SPL1-3 K1-K2). The sensitivity of the different numbers of Kato-Katz slides examined (black bars) was calculated in relation to the reference standard, which included the combined results of 18 Kato-Katz slides, the saline gradient, and the Helmintex methods.

Black circles indicate the prevalence profile in the population considering the sum of all parasitological methods used (reference standard: 18 Kato-Katz slides, saline gradient, and Helmintex); grey squares indicate the prevalence profile considering the recommended two KK slides from one fecal sample. Prevalence values (%) for each age group are indicated.

Using the reference standard, we found that children and young adults (11–20 years of age) had the highest prevalence (55%) for S. mansoni infection. In contrast, the prevalence was reduced to less than 50% in the other age groups, being further reduced in the elderly (older than 60 years of age). Importantly, the prevalence found in each age group, considering the combination of all parasitological exams (reference standard), was 1.7 to 4.7 times higher than the prevalence obtained with the recommended two KK slides from one fecal sample (Fig 4).
333.Rationale: The passage provides direct evidence that the standard diagnostic method (two Kato-Katz slides from one fecal sample) significantly underestimates the true prevalence of S. mansoni infection in this community. The study established a reference standard using multiple parasitological methods (18 Kato-Katz slides, saline gradient, and Helmintex) and found that the prevalence detected by this comprehensive approach was 45.9%, while the standard method detected only 20.4%. This represents a 2.3-fold increase. Additionally, when analyzing age groups, the reference standard detected 1.7 to 4.7 times higher prevalence than the standard method. The highest discrepancy (4.7 times) was observed in some age groups, directly supporting the option's claim. The passage also explains that this underestimation is due to the low sensitivity of the standard method for detecting low-intensity infections, which are common in communities with long-term control interventions. 334.Option: The hyaluronidase from Loxosceles venom is essential for the activation of phospholipase-D, thereby enabling the toxin to exert its dermonecrotic effects.
Evidence: The molecular mechanism by which brown spider venom induces gravitational spreading of skin lesions and systemic involvement is not fully understood.
A fundamental requirement for venom to induce local spreading of lesions and systemic involvement is the presence of venom components that are able to degrade tissue barriers.
The delivery of venom toxins to neighboring bite sites and into systemic circulation is assisted by molecules that degrade extracellular matrix constituents such as proteases and hyaluronidases.

The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators.

We sought to develop dermonecrosis experiments using rabbit skin to determine the in vivo involvement of brown spider venom hyaluronidase in envenomation. Moreover, we aimed to observe the relationship of hyaluronidases in the skin deleterious activities of whole venom.

Injections of L. intermedia crude venom, recombinant dermonecrotic toxin [33] (positive controls), and dermonecrotic toxin plus Dietrich's Hyaluronidase all induced macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis and dermonecrosis in rabbit skin, which was followed by 3, 6 and 24 h of observation.

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h.
The macroscopic gravitational spreading and edema induced by the two recombinant enzymes injected together were very similar to whole venom.

Light microscopic analysis of rabbit skin biopsies 24 h after of the experimentally induced dermonecrosis experiments performed above suggested that recombinant hyaluronidase was able to disorganize the extracellular matrix from rabbit skin dermis.
Dermonecrotic toxin alone triggered typical inflammatory cell accumulation, as well as collagen fiber disorganization and presence of fibrin in connective tissue.
These inflammatory events, developed by dermonecrotic toxin, were intensified when combined with Dietrich's Hyaluronidase, supporting an extensive edema and spreading of lesion.
334.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that hyaluronidase is essential for the activation of phospholipase-D. Instead, it describes hyaluronidase as a 'spreading factor' that facilitates the diffusion of other venom toxins through the extracellular matrix. The experiments show that hyaluronidase enhances the effects of dermonecrotic toxins but do not indicate a direct role in activating phospholipase-D. The conclusion about activation is not supported by the evidence provided.

The idea that brown spider venom hyaluronidases play a role in the gravitational spreading of dermonecrosis and/or systemic diffusion of venom toxins, and then act as “spreading factors” comes from its degradative activity on hyaluronic acid and other glycosaminoglycans that mediate tissue integrity and stability.

By degrading glycosaminoglycans, the hyaluronidase reduces the viscosity of hyaluronic acid and renders the extracellular matrix less rigid. This change makes the matrix more permeable to other toxins and facilitates the spread of other venom constituents and inflammatory cell mediators.

We sought to develop dermonecrosis experiments using rabbit skin to determine the in vivo involvement of brown spider venom hyaluronidase in envenomation. Moreover, we aimed to observe the relationship of hyaluronidases in the skin deleterious activities of whole venom.

Injections of L. intermedia crude venom, recombinant dermonecrotic toxin [33] (positive controls), and dermonecrotic toxin plus Dietrich's Hyaluronidase all induced macroscopic erythema, ecchymosis and dermonecrosis in rabbit skin, which was followed by 3, 6 and 24 h of observation.

The length of dermonecrosis lesions in the rabbit skin of Dietrich's Hyaluronidase concomitantly injected with dermonecrotic toxin was substantially larger than for dermonecrotic toxin alone after 24 h.
The macroscopic gravitational spreading and edema induced by the two recombinant enzymes injected together were very similar to whole venom.

Light microscopic analysis of rabbit skin biopsies 24 h after of the experimentally induced dermonecrosis experiments performed above suggested that recombinant hyaluronidase was able to disorganize the extracellular matrix from rabbit skin dermis.
Dermonecrotic toxin alone triggered typical inflammatory cell accumulation, as well as collagen fiber disorganization and presence of fibrin in connective tissue.
These inflammatory events, developed by dermonecrotic toxin, were intensified when combined with Dietrich's Hyaluronidase, supporting an extensive edema and spreading of lesion.
334.Rationale: The passage does not state that hyaluronidase is primarily responsible for the direct induction of dermonecrotic lesions and systemic toxicity. Instead, it describes hyaluronidase as a 'spreading factor' that enhances the effects of other venom toxins. The experiments show that hyaluronidase alone does not induce dermonecrotic lesions, but when combined with dermonecrotic toxins, it increases the severity and spread of the lesions. The direct induction of dermonecrotic lesions is attributed to other venom components, not to hyaluronidase acting alone. The conclusion that hyaluronidase is primarily responsible is not supported by the evidence provided.

335.Option: The higher odds of cysticercosis in breeding female pigs compared to males indicates that reproductive status directly increases susceptibility to Taenia spp. infection.
Evidence: The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01), and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: The passage states that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs, but it does not provide evidence that reproductive status directly increases susceptibility to Taenia spp. infection. The observed association could be due to other factors, such as differences in behavior, management practices, or exposure to contaminated environments. The passage does not establish a direct causal relationship between reproductive status and susceptibility to infection.

335.Option: Environmental factors, including land cover and precipitation, influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections in humans and pigs, with flooding agricultural and grassland areas showing a non-linear association with cysticercosis risk.
Evidence: The majority of environmental covariates, when included in a univariable model as non-linear terms, did not significantly improve model fit in comparison to inclusion as linear terms (Tables A and B in S3 File). A non-linear relationship (quadratic) was indicated between the percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland, and presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans (p = 0.04). Thus, subsequent analysis of human data included the percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland as a quadratic term.
Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).

The final model for presence of Taenia spp. antigen in pigs (Table 5) indicated that breeding sows had significantly higher odds of antigen presence compared to male pigs (OR = 10.35, p = 0.01), and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome (OR = 1.04, p = 0.004).
335.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that environmental factors, including land cover and precipitation, influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections in humans and pigs. Specifically, it states that a non-linear relationship was indicated between the percentage of land that was flooding agricultural and grassland and the presence of Taenia spp. antigen in humans. Additionally, precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome, and flooding agricultural land and grassland demonstrated a positive association with the outcome in pigs. These findings support the inference that environmental factors influence the spatial distribution of Taenia spp. infections.

335.Option: The spatial clustering of cysticercosis cases in households near tapeworm carriers is the primary driver of infection risk, overshadowing the influence of environmental factors.
Evidence: Spatial analyses have demonstrated significant clustering of taeniasis, porcine cysticercosis and human cysticercosis, with evidence of aggregation of human and porcine cysticercosis cases within close proximity to human tapeworm carriers [15–18].

The influence of socio-economic, behavioural and environmental variables was assessed for human and porcine cysticercosis. Evidence for the influence of environmental factors on the distribution of Taenia spp. infections may provide the basis for further epidemiological research, to support the development and targeting of control programmes.
335.Rationale: The passage mentions that spatial clustering of cysticercosis cases in households near tapeworm carriers has been observed, but it does not state that this is the primary driver of infection risk that overshadows the influence of environmental factors. In fact, the passage indicates that the influence of environmental factors was assessed and found to be significant in the study. The passage does not provide evidence to support the claim that spatial clustering near tapeworm carriers is the primary driver of infection risk.

335.Option: The lack of correlation between microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA results in this study suggests that the copro-antigen ELISA is unreliable for detecting taeniasis, and thus, the observed spatial patterns in cysticercosis may be influenced by diagnostic inaccuracies.
Evidence: The correlation between the two diagnostic methods for taeniasis (microscopy and copro-antigen ELISA) was zero: four participants were positive for taeniasis via microscopy only, 397 were positive for taeniasis via copro-antigen detection only and none were positive using both methods.

The prevalence of taeniasis, based on direct observation of Taenia spp. eggs (i.e. using only the microscopy results), was 0.2% (95% confidence interval [CI]: 0.05%–0.5%, note that this includes both T. saginata and T. solium and the methods used cannot differentiate between them). Based on the lack of correlation between the two diagnostic methods, and unexpectedly high number of positive results, the taeniasis results were not used in further statistical analyses as a precautionary measure.
335.Rationale: The passage states that the correlation between the two diagnostic methods for taeniasis was zero, and that the taeniasis results were not used in further statistical analyses as a precautionary measure. However, the passage does not suggest that the copro-antigen ELISA is unreliable for detecting taeniasis. The lack of correlation could be due to various factors, such as differences in sensitivity and specificity of the two methods, or the presence of cross-reactivity. The passage does not provide evidence to support the claim that the observed spatial patterns in cysticercosis may be influenced by diagnostic inaccuracies.

335.Option: The negative association between precipitation and cysticercosis risk suggests that rainfall reduces environmental contamination, thereby decreasing transmission of Taenia spp. eggs.
Evidence: Precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (OR = 0.998, p = 0.02).

Egg survival is influenced by temperature and humidity, with tropical regions being particularly suitable for transmission [10]. Surface moisture and humidity are thought to be the main constraining factors for Taenia spp. eggs in the environment: the eggs are vulnerable to desiccation and survival is greatly reduced under dry conditions, regardless of temperature [11].
335.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that precipitation was negatively associated with the outcome (cysticercosis risk). Additionally, it states that egg survival is influenced by temperature and humidity, with tropical regions being particularly suitable for transmission. Surface moisture and humidity are thought to be the main constraining factors for Taenia spp. eggs in the environment, with survival greatly reduced under dry conditions. These findings support the inference that the negative association between precipitation and cysticercosis risk suggests that rainfall reduces environmental contamination, thereby decreasing transmission of Taenia spp. eggs.

336.Option: The high confidence in domestically-made vaccines and the preference for them over foreign-made vaccines indicate that public trust in China’s vaccine regulatory system has been fully restored after past scandals.
Evidence: The vast majority of the study participants reported that they were confident (48.7%, 95%CI 47.0 to 50.3) or completely confident (46.1%, 95%CI 44.5 to 47.8) in domestically-made COVID-19 vaccines.
In contrast, 20.4% (95%CI 19.1 to 21.8) reported that they were completely confident and 57.4% (95%CI 55.8 to 59.1) reported that they were confident in foreign-made vaccines.

Findings on the preference of domestically-made over foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines revealed that 64.2% (95%CI 62.6 to 65.8) reported a preference for domestically-made and 11.9% (95%CI 10.8 to 13.0) preferred foreign-made COVID-19 vaccines.
336.Rationale: The passage provides evidence of high confidence in domestically-made vaccines (46.1% completely confident, 48.7% confident) and a strong preference for them (64.2%). However, the claim that 'public trust in China’s vaccine regulatory system has been fully restored' is an overstatement. The passage also mentions that 'vaccine scandals and a series of reports about the serious side-effects of vaccination have increased vaccination hesitancy and distrust in the country’s immunization program' in the past decade. The current confidence levels do not necessarily indicate that trust has been 'fully restored' to pre-scandal levels. The evidence supports high confidence but does not confirm full restoration of trust.

336.Option: The perception of susceptibility to COVID-19 was not a significant predictor of vaccination intention, indicating that public health campaigns should focus on increasing fear of infection to drive vaccine uptake.
Evidence: Most of the constructs in the HBM model were significantly associated with having a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination in the univariate analysis.
Perceived susceptibility refers to beliefs regarding vulnerability to infection, while perceived severity refers to beliefs regarding the negative effects of contracting the infection.

The participants had low perceptions of susceptibility. The majority disagreed that there was a great chance of getting COVID-19 in the next few months (68.7%), were also not worried about the likelihood of getting COVID-19 (56.8%), and disagreed that it was currently possible that they would get COVID-19 (72.0%).
336.Rationale: The passage states that 'most of the constructs in the HBM model were significantly associated with having a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination in the univariate analysis,' which includes perceived susceptibility. While the participants had low perceptions of susceptibility, the option incorrectly claims that perception of susceptibility was 'not a significant predictor.' The passage does not state that perceived susceptibility was not a significant predictor in the multivariable analysis. The suggestion to 'increase fear of infection' is not supported by the passage, which does not discuss the effectiveness of fear-based campaigns.

336.Option: The significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship between perceived susceptibility, severity, and cues to action with WTP suggests that public health interventions should prioritize higher-income groups to maximize vaccine uptake.
Evidence: The results of the moderating effect of income level on the relationship between the constructs of HBM and WTP are shown in Table 4. A significant positive moderating effect of income level on the relationship perceived susceptibility and WTP was found (β = 3.007, p = 0.045). Income level was also found significantly moderated the relationship between perceived severity and WTP (β = 3.980, p = 0.026).
There was a gradual increase in the odds of WTP for an amount of CNY¥500 [US$72] over CNY¥100/200 [US$14/28] with an increase in household income.
336.Rationale: The passage confirms a significant positive moderating effect of income on the relationship between perceived susceptibility, severity, and WTP. However, the option incorrectly infers that public health interventions should 'prioritize higher-income groups to maximize vaccine uptake.' The passage does not suggest that higher-income groups should be prioritized for vaccine distribution. The evidence supports that higher-income individuals are willing to pay more, but this does not imply that public health interventions should prioritize them for vaccine uptake. The option misinterprets the relationship between WTP and vaccine distribution priorities.

336.Option: The fact that only 28.7% of respondents reported a definite intention to vaccinate suggests that the majority of the population in China is unwilling to accept a COVID-19 vaccine, necessitating mandatory vaccination policies.
Evidence: On the whole, a total of 2,950 (83.5%; 95%CI 82.3–84.8) participants responded yes to COVID-19 vaccine intent, while only 582 (16.4%; 95%CI 15.2–17.7) responded no.
By a more specific breakdown, the majority responded probably yes (54.8%; 95% CI 53.2–56.5) followed by definitely yes (28.7%; 95% CI 27.3–30.3%).
336.Rationale: The passage states that 83.5% of participants reported vaccine intent (including 'probably yes' and 'definitely yes'), with 28.7% reporting 'definitely yes.' The option incorrectly claims that 'the majority of the population in China is unwilling to accept a COVID-19 vaccine,' which is not supported by the data. The passage does not suggest that mandatory vaccination policies are necessary, as the overall vaccine intent is high. The option misrepresents the data and draws an unsupported conclusion about the need for mandatory policies.

336.Option: Despite concerns about the safety and efficacy of the vaccine, the perception of benefits and the provision of adequate information were strong predictors of a definite intention to receive the COVID-19 vaccine.
Evidence: Perceived benefits of a new COVID-19 vaccine under the perceived barriers construct were among the strong strongest significant correlates of having a definite intention for COVID-19 vaccination.
Perception that vaccination decreases the chances of getting COVID-19 under the perceived benefit construct (OR = 3.14, 95% CI 2.05–4.83) was the strongest predictor for a definite intention.
Participants who disagreed with taking the vaccine unless it had been taken by many in the public were associated with having a higher definite intention to vaccinate (OR = 1.88, 95% CI 1.52–2.33).
Concerns about the COVID-19 vaccine being faulty/fake and affordability were reported by 82.0% and 75.1%, respectively. Concerns about safety (72.6%) and efficacy (71.2%) were also notable.
336.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that despite concerns about safety and efficacy, the perception of benefits (e.g., reducing the risk of infection and complications) was a strong predictor of definite vaccination intention. The provision of adequate information was also identified as a cue to action, with 92.1% of participants reporting they would only receive the vaccine if given adequate information. The evidence supports the conclusion that perception of benefits and adequate information are strong predictors of vaccination intention, even in the presence of concerns about safety and efficacy.

337.Option: The multivariate regression model incorporating HI and socioeconomic/climatic variables is superior to simulation models in predicting DHF incidence, suggesting that real-world data can fully capture the dynamics of antibody-dependent enhancement.
Evidence: Although incorporation of socioeconomic/variables improved the goodness of fit, this multivariate predictive model still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence (compare Figure 2B vs Figure 5).

The goodness of fit in predicting incidence by R0 showed remarkable differences between simulation with cross-immunity and those without cross-immunity (Figure 8). The predictability was much worse in simulation with cross-immunity than in other simulations.
337.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the multivariate regression model failed to reproduce the wide variation in observed incidence and that the predictability was worse in simulations with cross-immunity. This directly contradicts the claim that the multivariate model is superior to simulation models. Furthermore, the passage does not support the assertion that real-world data can 'fully capture' ADE dynamics, as the simulations with cross-immunity (which model ADE) showed different predictability patterns.

337.Option: The observed non-monotonic relationship between DHF incidence and HI is primarily driven by age-dependent differences in the probability of manifesting severe illness, independent of cross-serotype immunity.
Evidence: The present study aims to provide an empirical example of this non-monotonic relationship between the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity, with transmission intensity represented by vector abundance.
The simulations with cross-immunity (which models ADE) showed a non-monotonic relationship, while simulations without cross-immunity showed different patterns (Figure 7).
337.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the non-monotonic relationship is not independent of cross-serotype immunity. The simulation results show that the non-monotonic pattern is specifically associated with the cross-immunity scenario (which models ADE). The option incorrectly claims the relationship is 'independent of cross-serotype immunity,' which contradicts the evidence presented.

337.Option: The negative response of DHF incidence to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission has important public health implications, regardless of its underlying mechanism.
Evidence: The present study aims to provide an empirical example of this non-monotonic relationship between the incidence of DHF and transmission intensity, with transmission intensity represented by vector abundance.
The negative response of DHF incidence to transmission intensity at areas of intense transmission has important public health implications, regardless of its underlying mechanism.
337.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the negative response of DHF incidence to transmission intensity at high transmission levels has important public health implications. This is directly supported by the study's aim to demonstrate this non-monotonic relationship and the discussion of its implications for dengue control programs. The option correctly captures this conclusion without requiring knowledge of the specific mechanism.

337.Option: Since HI is strongly correlated with vector abundance, reducing HI to below 30 will inevitably lead to a significant reduction in DHF incidence across all transmission settings.
Evidence: Univariate analysis of GAM revealed that the correlation between HI and incidence was positive below about HI = 30, while the correlation was negative above this HI value (Figure 2B; Figure 3).
As HI decreases from 70 to 30, for example, the log incidence would increase by 0.35 (Figure 3), which is equivalent to an increase of 40% in incidence, since exp (0.35) = 1.4.
337.Rationale: The passage clearly shows that the relationship between HI and DHF incidence is non-monotonic. Reducing HI below 30 actually leads to an increase in DHF incidence, as demonstrated by the data. The option incorrectly assumes a linear relationship and makes an absolute claim ('inevitably lead to a significant reduction') that is contradicted by the evidence presented.

337.Option: The high variability in DHF incidence across districts is primarily due to differences in the number of public large water wells, which serve as the main breeding sites for Aedes mosquitoes.
Evidence: Although incorporation of socioeconomic/variables improved the goodness of fit, this multivariate predictive model still failed to reproduce the very wide variation in the observed incidence (compare Figure 2B vs Figure 5).

The final regression model obtained from the multivariate GAM analysis (Table 1) is presented. Log incidence of DHF reported between 2002 to 2004 was regressed against House Index and socioeconomic/variables. The per capital number of public large wells was assigned with df = 3, while other covariates were assigned with df = 2.
337.Rationale: The passage states that even with the inclusion of socioeconomic variables (including public large water wells), the multivariate model still failed to explain the wide variation in DHF incidence. This directly contradicts the claim that the variability is 'primarily due' to public large water wells. The evidence shows that while these variables contribute, they do not account for the majority of the observed variability.

338.Option: The 'valley of death' in diagnostic development is primarily caused by insufficient funding for large-scale prospective studies, which prevents promising early-phase candidates from being validated in real-world settings.
Evidence: Eighty-six out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.
Seven tests (6 antigens) in phase III are evaluated on less than 600 specimens, while 3 antigens/tests are evaluated on 601 to 5000 specimens and no antigen is evaluated on >5000 specimens.
338.Rationale: While the passage does show that most tests remain in early phases and few reach large-scale phase III studies, it does not provide evidence about the cause of this pattern. The term 'valley of death' is not mentioned, and the passage does not discuss funding as a limiting factor. The conclusion about insufficient funding is an external assumption not supported by the passage.

338.Option: The diagnostic accuracy of rK28-based immunochromatographic tests in East Africa is consistently high across multiple studies, suggesting that this antigen is a reliable candidate for widespread deployment in the region.
Evidence: RDTs with rK28 antigen have been evaluated in four studies in Sudan and Ethiopia with promising results for the test manufactured by CTK Biotech (sensitivities between 94 and 98%, and specificities between 95 and 98%) while sensitivities and specificities based on RDTs by other manufacturers and in ELISA were more variable (sensitivities between 89 and 100%, and specificities between 81 and 99%).

With rk28 having been evaluated on a relatively small number of specimens (VL suspected patients: 285), there is a risk that its performance turns out to be variable based on the population it is evaluated on.
338.Rationale: The passage states that rK28-based tests show promising results but also highlights variability in performance across manufacturers and the risk of variable performance in different populations. The conclusion that it is a 'reliable candidate for widespread deployment' is not supported by the evidence, as the passage explicitly notes limitations in the current evaluation data.

338.Option: The diagnostic development pipeline for visceral leishmaniasis serodiagnostic tests is characterized by a high proportion of early-phase evaluations, with most candidates not advancing to large-scale prospective studies despite showing high diagnostic accuracy in initial assessments.
Evidence: Eighty-six out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.

Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed (green in Fig 2 and grey in Table 1).
338.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that 89% of tests remain in phase I and II, and that some of these early-phase tests show high diagnostic accuracy. This directly supports the conclusion that many promising candidates do not advance to large-scale studies despite initial success. The pattern described in the option is clearly reflected in the data presented.

338.Option: The majority of serodiagnostic tests for visceral leishmaniasis are not advanced to phase III because they fail to meet minimum diagnostic accuracy thresholds in early-phase evaluations.
Evidence: Seventeen of the tests in phase I, 11 in phase II and 1 test in phase III (rK28 immunochromatographic test [ICT] from CTK) consistently have sensitivities above 94% and specificities above 97% in all studies analysed (green in Fig 2 and grey in Table 1).
Eighty-six out of the 96 tests (89%) were evaluated in phase I and/or II only.
338.Rationale: The passage shows that many tests with high diagnostic accuracy in early phases do not advance to phase III, not that they fail to meet accuracy thresholds. The option incorrectly attributes the lack of advancement to failure in early-phase accuracy, when the data suggest that even high-performing tests often do not progress to large-scale studies.

338.Option: The lack of standardized production methods for native antigens with carbohydrate and lipid moieties contributes to high batch-to-batch variation, which limits their potential for commercialization and widespread use in low-resource settings.
Evidence: Fifteen antigens contain both a carbohydrate and a lipid moiety as they are made from whole parasites, whole parasite lysates, the entire secretome, the parasite membrane or lipophosphoglycan (LPG).
The second part of the analysis of the antigen characteristics is summarized in Fig 3. All 20 antigens with a known carbohydrate and or lipid moiety are native antigens.
338.Rationale: The passage describes the composition of native antigens but does not discuss production methods, batch-to-batch variation, or commercialization challenges. The option introduces a causal relationship not supported by the evidence in the passage.

339.Option: Participants who reported trying to do as many physical and social activities as possible were more likely to experience a faster recovery and return to normal functioning, suggesting that active coping strategies are clinically effective.
Evidence: Participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible. For example, they tried to mobilize by performing the physical exercises or movements they could do. With this in mind, people were more willing to accept that symptoms would eventually subside.
339.Rationale: The passage does not provide evidence that active coping strategies (trying physical/social activities) led to faster recovery or return to normal functioning. It only states that individuals who used emotional coping strategies engaged in these activities and were more willing to accept that symptoms would eventually subside. There is no mention of clinical effectiveness or faster recovery times for these individuals.

339.Option: Since effective long-term treatments are not available yet for chronic chikungunya, the high uptake of (alternative) medicines and frequent physician visits will normally not lead to success, resulting in dissatisfaction and further emotional stress.
Evidence: Frequent physician visits and different medical treatments was a pattern of problem-focused coping, which was illustrative for people who perceived severe impairment from chikungunya, which they could hardly accept. Frequent visits of health care providers as coping strategy led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress (negative impact on QoL).

Some of the participants did not visit a physician. These participants did not recognize visiting a physician as a problem-focused coping strategy, because they would not be treated with more than the regular symptom relief-providing medications, i.e. paracetamol and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.
339.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that frequent physician visits and use of alternative medicines (problem-focused coping strategies) led to unmet expectations for cure or treatment, resulting in stress and negative impact on quality of life. This directly supports the inference that these strategies typically do not lead to success when effective long-term treatments are unavailable, resulting in dissatisfaction and emotional stress.

339.Option: The universal health care program in Curaçao minimized out-of-pocket expenditures and reduced the financial burden on individuals, which may have contributed to higher healthcare utilization among chikungunya patients.
Evidence: The financial impact due to chikungunya on participant’s personal situation was generally perceived as limited. In Curaçao, chikungunya patients could rely on an insurance system, which covered health care costs, which in turn minimized out-of-pocket expenditures. These insurances covered also parts of the income loss of chikungunya patients who could not work.
339.Rationale: The passage explicitly states that the insurance system in Curaçao covered health care costs and minimized out-of-pocket expenditures for chikungunya patients. This directly supports the inference that the universal health care program reduced financial burden and likely contributed to higher healthcare utilization among patients.

339.Option: Problem-focused coping strategies aimed at physical healing induced a high uptake of (alternative) medicines and consultation of physicians, whereas emotional coping strategies focused on acceptance of the situation were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less uptake of medical care.
Evidence: Within the community, problem-focused coping was commonly performed by using (natural) medicines and by visiting a physician to cope with the physical impact of chikungunya.
Participants using emotional coping strategies went less often to a physician, used less medications and reported trying to do as many physical/social activities as possible.

Frequent physician visits and different medical treatments was a pattern of problem-focused coping, which was illustrative for people who perceived severe impairment from chikungunya, which they could hardly accept.
339.Rationale: The passage provides clear evidence that problem-focused coping strategies (using alternative medicines and consulting physicians) were associated with higher healthcare utilization, while emotional coping strategies (acceptance) were linked to lower perceived impacts on HRQoL and less medical care use. This directly supports the inference presented in the option.

340.Option: The increase in the median age of hospitalized dengue cases over time indicates that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred to hospitals.
Evidence: The median age of dengue cases remained about the same from 2007 onwards (Fig 3). On the other hand, the median age among hospitalized cases increased from 38 years in 2007 to 43 years in 2017 (p < 0.0005) (Fig 3).
340.Rationale: The passage states that the median age of dengue cases remained about the same from 2007 onwards, but the median age among hospitalized cases increased from 38 to 43 years. This suggests that older patients are being hospitalized more frequently, not that younger patients are being under-treated or under-referred. The increase in median age of hospitalized cases could be due to more selective admission criteria favoring older patients who are at higher risk of severe disease, rather than under-treatment of younger patients.

340.Option: The reduction in hospitalization rates for dengue cases is primarily due to improved early diagnosis and management in primary care settings, which has led to fewer patients requiring hospital admission.
Evidence: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).

The hospitalization rates of persons suspected of dengue viral infection are high, as doctors tend to err on the side of caution and admit them for monitoring. This leads to higher bed occupancy rates, although majority of dengue cases are unlikely to require or benefit from medical care in hospital for their mild form of illness.

In the aftermath of the 2004–2005 dengue epidemic in Singapore, hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria for dengue cases [11–13]. The Singapore Ministry of Health (MOH) sent out circulars to hospitals, government primary care clinics and medical practitioners to apprise them of the dengue situation during epidemic periods, and to provide periodic updates on guidelines for the management of dengue [14–19].
340.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the reduction in hospitalization rates is primarily due to the implementation of more selective admission criteria by hospitals and MOH guidelines, not primarily due to improved early diagnosis and management in primary care. The passage explicitly states that hospitals reviewed and refined their admission criteria after the 2004-2005 epidemic, and MOH provided updated guidelines. The high hospitalization rates in the past were due to doctors erring on the side of caution, not due to a lack of early diagnosis in primary care.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria for dengue has not led to an increase in adverse outcomes, despite a significant reduction in the proportion of cases hospitalized.
Evidence: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized plummeted from 97.3% in 2003 to an all-time low of 25.6% in 2014 (p < 0.0005).
The proportion of deaths among hospitalized patients ranged from 0.14% in 2003 to 0.77% in 2009.
The CFR ranged from 0% in 2017 to 0.27% in 2006.
340.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the implementation of more selective admission criteria has not led to an increase in adverse outcomes. The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized has significantly decreased, but the case fatality rate (CFR) has not increased. In fact, the CFR was 0% in 2017, the lowest in the study period. The proportion of deaths among hospitalized patients also remained relatively low, ranging from 0.14% to 0.77%. This suggests that the more selective admission criteria have not compromised patient outcomes.

340.Option: The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized in the elderly aged ≥65 years has been highest since 2013, which may be attributed to both the MOH guideline to refer for hospital evaluation those aged ≥65 years as well as a shift in demographic profile of dengue cases towards older adults.
Evidence: The highest proportions were in older adults aged 45–64 years from 2003 to 2012 (range: 50.8% to 100.0%) and elderly persons aged ≥65 years from 2013 to 2017 (range: 47.2% to 55.4%).
In the majority of years after 2007, the hospitalization rate per 100,000 population was highest in the elderly ≥65 years of age.

The Singapore Ministry of Health (MOH) sent out circulars to hospitals, government primary care clinics and medical practitioners to apprise them of the dengue situation during epidemic periods, and to provide periodic updates on guidelines for the management of dengue [14–19]. In 2007, a medical expert committee established the criteria for immediate referral to hospitals, which comprised objective criteria such as significant bleeding, fall in blood pressure, dehydration and/or postural hypotension, a rise in the haematocrit ≥ 20% above the baseline and platelet count < 80 000 cells / mm3, as well as subjective criteria such as severe vomiting or diarrhoea, severe abdominal pain, and elderly patients with medical co-morbidities who are unwell [15].
340.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the proportion of dengue cases hospitalized in the elderly aged ≥65 years has been highest since 2013. The passage also mentions that MOH guidelines include criteria for immediate referral to hospitals for elderly patients with medical co-morbidities who are unwell. This suggests that the higher hospitalization rate in the elderly may be due to both the MOH guidelines and a possible shift in the demographic profile of dengue cases towards older adults.

340.Option: The implementation of more selective admission criteria by hospitals has led to a significant reduction in hospital bed utilization, even during large dengue epidemics.
Evidence: The total number of hospital bed-years due to dengue halved from 136 in 2005 to 69 in 2013 (Fig 4).
The proportion of dengue cases hospitalized during these three epidemic periods was 93.2% in 2004–2005, 58.1% in 2007 and 28.9% in 2013–2014.

The hospitalization rates of persons suspected of dengue viral infection are high, as doctors tend to err on the side of caution and admit them for monitoring. This leads to higher bed occupancy rates, although majority of dengue cases are unlikely to require or benefit from medical care in hospital for their mild form of illness.
340.Rationale: The passage provides evidence that the implementation of more selective admission criteria has led to a significant reduction in hospital bed utilization, even during large dengue epidemics. The total number of hospital bed-years due to dengue halved from 2005 to 2013. During the 2013-2014 epidemic, the proportion of dengue cases hospitalized was 28.9%, a significant decrease from 93.2% in the 2004-2005 epidemic. This suggests that the more selective admission criteria have effectively reduced hospital bed utilization, even during large epidemics.

341.Option: The decline in anti-filarial IgG4 antibody prevalence after MDA occurred more slowly than the decline in microfilaria prevalence, indicating that antibody levels persist longer after infection clearance and may reflect past exposure rather than current transmission.

Antibody clearance tends to occur faster after treatment in children than in adults, since children tend to have shorter term exposure and lighter infections. Therefore, adults may have persistent antibodies years after effective therapy.

The rebound in Ascaris prevalence is unrelated to filarial transmission. The passage does not provide evidence that transmission of B. timori was not fully interrupted.

We used the Brugia Rapid dipstick test as a marker for ongoing or prior infection, because there is no adult worm antigen detection test for Brugia spp.

The sequence variation in mitochondrial genes ranges from 24.6–47.4%, with an overall mt genome difference of 32.87%. Reproductive isolation would require additional evidence such as attempts at cross-breeding or analysis of reproductive compatibility, which are not addressed in the passage.

343.Option: The cumulative negative conversion rate of blood-cultured fungi is significantly influenced by the duration of antifungal therapy, with longer treatment leading to better clearance regardless of fungal susceptibility.

The option incorrectly assumes a direct relationship between treatment duration and clearance that is not supported by the study's findings.

344.Option: The Clone 13 vaccine is safe for use in pregnant ewes at any stage of gestation, as demonstrated by the absence of acute clinical signs in vaccinated animals.
Evidence: One ewe in the group vaccinated at approximately 50 days after mating showed signs of imminent abortion at 76 days after inoculation. The proportion of stillborn lambs was 30% for the group inoculated at approximately 50 days after mating (including the foetus that was about to be aborted) and 11% for the group inoculated at approximately 120 days after mating.

The option claiming safety at 'any stage of gestation' is directly contradicted by these findings. The proportion of stillborn lambs was 30% for the group inoculated at approximately 50 days after mating (including the foetus that was about to be aborted) and 11% for the group inoculated at approximately 120 days after mating.

Evidence: However, two other WB are commercially available worldwide. The Chagas blot and Abbott ESA Chagas, whose use is limited to Europe and the USA, respectively.

Evidence: Until 2015 and 2017, respectively, HBK 740 Immunoblot Linhas anti-T. cruzi and TESA-blot were routinely used for confirmatory testing; however, commercial productions have since been discontinued, leaving a gap in the performance of confirmatory chronic CD diagnosis.

345.Option: The absence of cross-reactivity with visceral leishmaniasis in this study implies that chimeric recombinant antigens are universally safe for use in all co-endemic regions, regardless of local parasite diversity.
Evidence: No cross-reactions were observed when assaying leishmaniasis-positive serum samples using all four chimeric proteins.

Until 2015 and 2017, respectively, HBK 740 Immunoblot Linhas anti-T. cruzi and TESA-blot were routinely used for confirmatory testing; however, commercial productions have since been discontinued, leaving a gap in the performance of confirmatory chronic CD diagnosis.

However, it also shows that patients with abdominal pain or diarrhea were less likely to have hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. The passage does not provide information about the severity of systemic disease or comorbidities in relation to gastrointestinal involvement.

The option introduces claims about hospital stay and disease severity that are not supported by the data presented in the passage.

346.Option: A quicker diagnosis through endoscopy may explain why patients with gastrointestinal identification of H. capsulatum had lower severity and case fatality compared to those without gastrointestinal symptoms.
Evidence: Those with gastrointestinal H. capsulatum had a shorter delay between symptoms onset and diagnosis.

Biopsies performed during colonoscopy were more contributive than those performed during oesogastroduodenoscopy whether considering direct examination (respectively 56.4% vs 21.7% positive), fungal culture (respectively 74.1% vs 40.9% positive), or pathology (respectively 72.9% vs 28.6% positive).

346.Option: The increasing prevalence of gastrointestinal manifestations over time is likely due to improved access to abdominal CT-scans and gastrointestinal biopsies, which enhances detection of subclinical cases.
Evidence: There was a linear trend for the odds of having gastrointestinal signs and symptoms which increased significantly with time (P<0.0001).

There was no such temporal trend for gastrointestinal endoscopies but there was a significant trend for an increase in the proportion of patients benefitting from and abdominal CT-scan, a gastrointestinal biopsy, and a decrease in abdominal ultrasonography.
346.Rationale: The passage shows a significant increase in gastrointestinal manifestations over time, while noting that gastrointestinal endoscopies did not show a temporal trend. However, there was a significant increase in abdominal CT-scans and gastrointestinal biopsies. This supports the inference that improved diagnostic access through these modalities likely contributed to the observed increase in gastrointestinal manifestations, as they would enhance detection of subclinical cases.

346.Option: The higher rate of positive identification via pathology in patients with gastrointestinal involvement suggests that histopathological examination is more sensitive than fungal culture or direct examination for detecting H. capsulatum in clinical samples.
Evidence: Among those having benefitted from gastrointestinal biopsies 65/117 (55%) had a positive identification of Histoplasma on the sample; 32 (27.3%) by direct examination, 44 (37.6) by culture, and 45 (38.4%) by pathology.
346.Rationale: The passage shows that pathology had the highest detection rate (38.4%) among the three methods, but it does not provide evidence to conclude that histopathological examination is more sensitive than fungal culture or direct examination in general. The comparison is limited to the specific context of gastrointestinal biopsies in this study. The option makes a broader claim about sensitivity that cannot be supported by the evidence provided.

347.Option: The presence of Anopheles mosquitoes in areas with high human population density is primarily driven by the availability of livestock blood rather than human blood, as human settlements are not a major attractant.
Evidence: An. sinensis is an outdoor resting and biting species, which mostly inhabits rice planting areas, and can be recorded between northeast to Liaoning Province and southwest to Yunnan Province.
An. lesteri is the main malaria vector at low altitudes in the 22°N to 33°N range.
An. minimus is active in southern China below 25°N and mainly inhabits marshes, ponds, and streams in jungle areas.
